topics.name: Cannabis Possession (Personal Use)
topics.description: How countries treat personal cannabis possession — from full legalization to death penalty.

CAN.note: Legal since 2018 under the Cannabis Act for adults 18+.

USA.note: Federally illegal but rescheduling underway; 24 states have legalized recreational use and many others have decriminalized.

GBR.note: Illegal as a Class B drug; possession can result in a warning, fine, or up to 5 years imprisonment, but penalties are often light for small amounts.

FRA.note: Illegal but a 2020 reform introduced a flat fine of EUR 200 for possession of small amounts.

DEU.note: Legal since April 2024 for adults; up to 25g possession in public and home cultivation of up to 3 plants allowed.

ITA.note: Personal use is decriminalized; possession of small amounts results in administrative sanctions rather than criminal charges.

ESP.note: Decriminalized for personal use in private spaces; cannabis clubs operate in a legal gray area.

PRT.note: Decriminalized since 2001; possession of up to 25g is an administrative offense, not a criminal one.

NLD.note: Technically illegal but tolerated under the gedoogbeleid policy; coffee shops may sell up to 5g per transaction.

BEL.note: Possession of up to 3g for personal use by adults is the lowest police priority and effectively decriminalized.

SWE.note: Illegal with strict zero-tolerance policy; any possession can result in fines or up to 6 months imprisonment.

NOR.note: Illegal but a 2021 reform proposal aimed at decriminalization was narrowly rejected; penalties for small amounts are usually fines.

DNK.note: Illegal; possession of small amounts typically results in a fine, though Christiania has historically tolerated open sales.

FIN.note: Illegal; possession of small amounts typically results in a fine rather than imprisonment.

ISL.note: Illegal; small-quantity possession is usually punished with fines.

CHE.note: Possession of up to 10g is decriminalized with a fine of CHF 100; pilot programs for legal sales began in 2023.

AUT.note: Personal use possession is decriminalized; small amounts result in diversion to treatment rather than criminal prosecution.

IRL.note: Illegal; first-time possession of small amounts is typically treated as a minor offense with a fine.

POL.note: Illegal; prosecutors may decline to prosecute for very small amounts deemed for personal use. Medical cannabis is legal.

CZE.note: Legal since January 2026 for adults 21+; up to 25g in public and 100g at home permitted.

HUN.note: Illegal with strict penalties; possession of even small amounts can lead to up to 2 years imprisonment.

ROU.note: Illegal with strict penalties; possession for personal use can result in 3 months to 2 years imprisonment.

BGR.note: Illegal; possession of any amount can result in 1 to 6 years imprisonment.

HRV.note: Possession of small amounts for personal use is a misdemeanor punishable by fine. Medical cannabis is legal.

GRC.note: Illegal but a 2017 law introduced lighter penalties for personal use; medical cannabis legalized in 2018.

TUR.note: Illegal with strict penalties; possession can result in 1 to 2 years imprisonment, with mandatory treatment programs.

RUS.note: Illegal; possession of up to 6g is an administrative offense with heavy fines, above that is a criminal offense with imprisonment.

UKR.note: Illegal; possession of small amounts (up to 5g) is an administrative offense with a fine. Medical cannabis legalized in 2024.

CHN.note: Illegal with strict penalties; possession can result in up to 15 days detention and forced rehabilitation programs.

JPN.note: Illegal with strict enforcement under the Cannabis Control Act; possession can result in up to 5 years imprisonment.

KOR.note: Illegal with strict penalties; possession can result in up to 5 years imprisonment. South Korea even prosecutes citizens for use abroad.

IND.note: Possession of small amounts (ganja/leaves) is punishable by up to 1 year imprisonment or fine; charas/resin carries stricter penalties.

PAK.note: Illegal but enforcement is inconsistent; penalties exist but cannabis use is culturally widespread in parts of the country.

BGD.note: Illegal under the Narcotics Control Act; possession can result in significant imprisonment.

IDN.note: Illegal with severe penalties; trafficking over 1kg can carry the death penalty, and personal possession can lead to lengthy imprisonment.

MYS.note: Illegal with severe penalties; trafficking over 200g carries a mandatory death sentence, though Malaysia ended the mandatory death penalty in 2023.

SGP.note: Illegal with severe penalties; trafficking over 500g carries a mandatory death penalty. Singapore executed multiple people for drug offenses in 2024.

THA.note: Decriminalized in 2022 when removed from the narcotics list; a regulatory framework is still being finalized.

VNM.note: Illegal with strict penalties; possession can result in imprisonment, and large-scale trafficking can carry the death penalty.

PHL.note: Illegal under the Comprehensive Dangerous Drugs Act; possession of any amount can result in 12 years to life imprisonment.

AUS.note: Varies by territory; the ACT legalized personal use in 2020, and most states have decriminalized or use diversion programs for small amounts.

NZL.note: Illegal; a 2020 referendum to legalize narrowly failed. Police discretion means small possession often results in warnings.

BRA.note: Decriminalized by the Supreme Court in 2024; possession of up to 40g is treated as a public health issue, not a criminal offense.

ARG.note: Personal use possession was effectively decriminalized by a 2009 Supreme Court ruling; medical cannabis is legal.

MEX.note: Supreme Court ruled prohibition unconstitutional in 2021; personal use of up to 5g is decriminalized. Full legalization stalled in Congress.

COL.note: Possession of up to 20g for personal use is decriminalized under the personal dose doctrine.

CHL.note: Personal use in private is decriminalized; public consumption remains an administrative offense.

ZAF.note: Constitutional Court ruled in 2018 that personal cultivation and use in private is legal.

NGA.note: Illegal under the National Drug Law Enforcement Agency Act; possession can result in a minimum of 15 years imprisonment.

EGY.note: Illegal with strict penalties; possession can result in fines and imprisonment of 1 to 5 years.

SAU.note: Illegal under Sharia law; trafficking can result in the death penalty, and possession carries severe prison sentences and corporal punishment.

ARE.note: Illegal with strict penalties; possession can result in a minimum of 4 years imprisonment, though recent reforms have slightly reduced sentences.

ISR.note: Decriminalized for personal use since 2017; fines for first and second offenses. Medical cannabis program is well established.

IRN.note: Illegal with severe penalties; trafficking large quantities can result in the death penalty. Iran leads the world in drug-related executions.

IRQ.note: Illegal with strict penalties; possession and use can result in imprisonment.

KEN.note: Illegal under the Narcotic Drugs and Psychotropic Substances Act; possession carries up to 20 years but enforcement varies.

ETH.note: Illegal; penalties include fines and imprisonment, though traditional use of khat is culturally accepted.

MAR.note: Illegal for personal use but widely cultivated; a 2021 law legalized cannabis for medical and industrial purposes.

URY.note: Fully legal since 2013; Uruguay was the first country in the world to fully legalize cannabis.

GEO.note: Constitutional Court ruled in 2018 that criminal punishment for personal use is unconstitutional.

MLT.note: Legal since 2021 for personal use; adults may possess up to 7g and grow up to 4 plants at home.

LUX.note: Legal since 2023 for personal use at home; adults may grow up to 4 plants.

JAM.note: Decriminalized in 2015; possession of up to 2 ounces is a petty offense with no criminal record.

LKA.note: Illegal with strict penalties; possession can result in imprisonment and heavy fines.

topics.name: Death Penalty
topics.description: Whether a country has abolished or retains capital punishment, and whether it is actively used.

CAN.note: Abolished in 1976 for ordinary crimes and fully in 1998.

USA.note: Retained at federal and state levels; 27 states authorize the death penalty and executions continue regularly.

GBR.note: Abolished for murder in 1965 and fully for all offenses in 1998.

FRA.note: Abolished in 1981; last execution was in 1977.

DEU.note: Abolished in 1949 under the Basic Law (constitution).

ITA.note: Abolished for ordinary crimes in 1947 and fully in 1994.

ESP.note: Abolished in 1978 under the post-Franco constitution; fully for all crimes in 1995.

PRT.note: Abolished in 1867 for ordinary crimes, one of the earliest countries to do so; fully in 1976.

NLD.note: Abolished for ordinary crimes in 1870 and fully in 1982.

BEL.note: Abolished fully in 1996; the last execution was in 1950.

SWE.note: Abolished for peacetime crimes in 1921 and fully in 1972.

NOR.note: Abolished for peacetime crimes in 1905 and fully in 1979.

DNK.note: Abolished for peacetime crimes in 1933 and fully in 1978.

FIN.note: Abolished for peacetime crimes in 1949 and fully in 1972.

ISL.note: Abolished fully in 1928; the last execution was in 1830.

CHE.note: Abolished for peacetime crimes in 1942 and fully in 1992.

AUT.note: Abolished fully in 1968; enshrined in the constitution.

IRL.note: Abolished for ordinary crimes in 1964 and fully in 1990.

POL.note: Abolished fully in 1997.

CZE.note: Abolished in 1990 as part of Czechoslovakia.

HUN.note: Abolished in 1990; declared unconstitutional by the Constitutional Court.

ROU.note: Abolished in 1989 after the fall of Ceausescu.

BGR.note: Abolished in 1998.

HRV.note: Abolished in 1990 upon independence.

GRC.note: Abolished for ordinary crimes in 1993 and fully in 2004.

TUR.note: Abolished for peacetime crimes in 2002 and fully in 2006 as part of EU accession reforms.

RUS.note: Official moratorium since 1996; the last execution was in 1996. The death penalty remains in law but is not applied.

UKR.note: Abolished in 2000; the Constitutional Court ruled it unconstitutional in 1999.

CHN.note: Retains and actively uses the death penalty for numerous offenses; estimated thousands executed annually, the highest number in the world.

JPN.note: Retains and uses the death penalty by hanging for murder; executions are carried out with little advance notice.

KOR.note: Retains the death penalty in law but has not carried out an execution since 1997; considered abolitionist in practice.

IND.note: Retains the death penalty for the 'rarest of rare' cases; executions are infrequent, with the last in 2020.

PAK.note: Retains and uses the death penalty; lifted a moratorium in 2014 and has since carried out hundreds of executions.

BGD.note: Retains and uses the death penalty for murder, terrorism, and drug offenses.

IDN.note: Retains the death penalty but has paused executions since 2016; sentences continue to be imposed for drug trafficking.

MYS.note: Abolished the mandatory death penalty in 2023; courts now have discretion, but capital punishment remains in law.

SGP.note: Retains and actively uses the death penalty, particularly for drug trafficking offenses. Multiple executions in 2024.

THA.note: Retains the death penalty but uses it rarely; the last execution was by lethal injection in 2018 after a 9-year hiatus.

VNM.note: Retains and uses the death penalty for drug trafficking, murder, and corruption offenses.

PHL.note: Abolished in 2006; attempts to reinstate it have so far been unsuccessful.

AUS.note: Abolished in all states by 1985 and at federal level in 2010.

NZL.note: Abolished for murder in 1961 and fully in 1989.

BRA.note: Abolished for ordinary crimes in 1882 and fully in 1988 under the current constitution.

ARG.note: Abolished fully in 2008.

MEX.note: Abolished fully in 2005; enshrined in the constitution.

COL.note: Abolished in 1910; one of the earliest Latin American countries to do so.

CHL.note: Abolished for ordinary crimes in 2001; retained only under military law in wartime.

ZAF.note: Abolished in 1995; the Constitutional Court ruled it unconstitutional in 1995.

NGA.note: Retains the death penalty; sentences are imposed but executions are rare. Some northern states apply Sharia-based capital punishment.

EGY.note: Retains and uses the death penalty for murder, terrorism, and drug trafficking; executions are regularly carried out.

SAU.note: Retains and actively uses the death penalty, often by beheading, for offenses including murder, drug trafficking, and apostasy.

ARE.note: Retains the death penalty but carries out executions rarely; sentences are sometimes commuted.

ISR.note: Retains the death penalty only for genocide, crimes against humanity, and treason in wartime; it has been used only once, in 1962 for Adolf Eichmann.

IRN.note: One of the world's leading executioners; the death penalty is used for murder, drug offenses, and morality crimes. At least 972 executions in 2024.

IRQ.note: Retains and uses the death penalty; executions are carried out for terrorism, murder, and kidnapping.

KEN.note: Retains the death penalty in law but has not carried out an execution since 1987; a de facto moratorium exists.

ETH.note: Retains the death penalty in law but has observed a de facto moratorium for over a decade.

MAR.note: Retains the death penalty in law but has not carried out an execution since 1993.

BLR.note: The only European country that retains and actively uses the death penalty; executions are carried out by shooting.

TWN.note: Retains the death penalty but use has declined significantly; the Constitutional Court ruled in 2024 to restrict its application.

MNG.note: Abolished in 2017 when the revised Criminal Code took effect.

ZWE.note: Abolished on 31 December 2024, the most recent country to do so.

topics.name: Abortion
topics.description: Legal status of abortion by country — from available on request to total prohibition.

CAN.note: No legal restrictions; abortion was decriminalized in 1988 and is available through the public healthcare system.

USA.note: Varies widely by state after Dobbs v. Jackson (2022); some states protect access while others impose near-total bans.

GBR.note: Legal on request up to 24 weeks under the Abortion Act 1967; available through the NHS.

FRA.note: Legal on request up to 14 weeks; right to abortion was enshrined in the Constitution in March 2024.

DEU.note: Legal on request in the first 12 weeks after mandatory counseling and a 3-day waiting period.

ITA.note: Legal on request up to 90 days under Law 194 (1978); however, access is limited by high rates of conscientious objection among doctors.

ESP.note: Legal on request up to 14 weeks; a 2023 reform removed the mandatory waiting period.

PRT.note: Legal on request up to 10 weeks since a 2007 referendum.

NLD.note: Legal on request up to 24 weeks; the mandatory 5-day waiting period was removed in 2023.

BEL.note: Legal on request up to 12 weeks after a 6-day reflection period.

SWE.note: Legal on request up to 18 weeks; between 18-21 weeks requires approval from the National Board of Health.

NOR.note: Legal on request up to 12 weeks; beyond that requires approval from a medical board.

DNK.note: Legal on request up to 12 weeks; available free of charge through the public health system.

FIN.note: Legal on request up to 12 weeks since a 2023 reform that removed the requirement for two doctors' approval.

ISL.note: Legal on request up to 22 weeks since a 2019 reform.

CHE.note: Legal on request during the first 12 weeks after counseling.

AUT.note: Legal on request during the first trimester (up to the end of the third month).

IRL.note: Legal on request up to 12 weeks since the 2018 referendum repealed the Eighth Amendment.

POL.note: Near-total ban since a 2020 Constitutional Tribunal ruling; only permitted when the pregnancy results from rape/incest or threatens the mother's life.

CZE.note: Legal on request up to 12 weeks; available as part of the national health system.

HUN.note: Legal on request up to 12 weeks after counseling, though recent regulations require listening to fetal heartbeat first.

ROU.note: Legal on request up to 14 weeks since 1989; previously restricted under Ceausescu's regime.

BGR.note: Legal on request up to 12 weeks; permitted on medical grounds up to 20 weeks.

HRV.note: Legal on request up to 10 weeks; access is sometimes limited by conscientious objection.

GRC.note: Legal on request up to 12 weeks; up to 24 weeks in cases of fetal abnormality.

TUR.note: Legal on request up to 10 weeks; spousal consent was previously required but is no longer enforced.

RUS.note: Legal on request up to 12 weeks; permitted later on medical or social grounds, though recent restrictions have narrowed access.

UKR.note: Legal on request up to 12 weeks; permitted on medical grounds up to 22 weeks.

CHN.note: Legal and widely available; historically part of population control policy, though recent pro-natalist policies discourage later abortions.

JPN.note: Legal with spousal consent up to 22 weeks on economic or health grounds under the Maternal Protection Act; not available on request alone.

KOR.note: Legal on request up to 14 weeks since the 2021 decriminalization following a 2019 Constitutional Court ruling.

IND.note: Legal up to 20 weeks on broad grounds under the Medical Termination of Pregnancy Act; extended to 24 weeks for special categories in 2021.

PAK.note: Legal only to save the woman's life or for necessary treatment in early pregnancy under Sharia-influenced law.

BGD.note: Technically illegal except to save the mother's life, but menstrual regulation procedures are available up to 12 weeks.

IDN.note: Illegal except in medical emergencies or for rape victims up to 40 days of gestation.

MYS.note: Legal when the pregnancy threatens the physical or mental health of the woman; relatively broadly interpreted in practice.

SGP.note: Legal on request up to 24 weeks for Singapore citizens and permanent residents.

THA.note: Legal on request up to 12 weeks since a 2021 law reform; available up to 20 weeks under certain conditions.

VNM.note: Legal on request; widely available with no gestational limit specified by law.

PHL.note: Total ban under all circumstances; criminalized under the Revised Penal Code with no exceptions.

AUS.note: Legal in all states and territories, though gestational limits and regulations vary by jurisdiction.

NZL.note: Legal on request up to 20 weeks since the 2020 Abortion Legislation Act.

BRA.note: Illegal except in cases of rape, risk to the mother's life, or anencephaly (since 2012).

ARG.note: Legal on request up to 14 weeks since the landmark 2020 law.

MEX.note: The Supreme Court decriminalized abortion nationwide in 2023; access still varies by state.

COL.note: Legal on request up to 24 weeks since a landmark 2022 Constitutional Court ruling.

CHL.note: Legal only in cases of risk to the mother's life, fatal fetal abnormality, or rape (since 2017).

ZAF.note: Legal on request up to 12 weeks under the Choice on Termination of Pregnancy Act (1996).

NGA.note: Illegal except to save the mother's life; some northern Sharia states further restrict access.

EGY.note: Illegal except when the mother's life is at risk; strong social and religious stigma.

SAU.note: Permitted in the first 120 days to preserve the mother's health or in cases of severe fetal abnormality with a medical committee's approval.

ARE.note: Permitted within 120 days if the pregnancy endangers the mother's life or if the fetus has severe abnormalities.

ISR.note: Legal with committee approval on various grounds including age, health, and marital status; approval is granted in the vast majority of cases.

IRN.note: Illegal except to save the mother's life or in cases of severe fetal abnormality before 4 months; restricted by religious rulings.

IRQ.note: Illegal except to save the mother's life.

KEN.note: Permitted when the life or health of the mother is at risk under the 2010 constitution, though implementation is inconsistent.

ETH.note: Legal on broad grounds since 2005 including rape, incest, fetal abnormality, physical or mental health, and for minors.

MAR.note: Illegal except to preserve the mother's physical health; reform has been debated but not enacted.

HND.note: Total ban with no exceptions; even emergency contraception is prohibited.

SLV.note: Total ban with no exceptions since 1998; women have been imprisoned for miscarriages suspected to be abortions.

NIC.note: Total ban with no exceptions since 2006.

MLT.note: Banned except when the mother's life or health is in serious jeopardy, following a 2023 reform.

topics.name: Same-Sex Marriage
topics.description: Legal recognition of same-sex relationships — from full marriage equality to criminalization of homosexuality.

CAN.note: Legal nationwide since 2005 under the Civil Marriage Act; fourth country to legalize.

USA.note: Legal nationwide since the 2015 Supreme Court ruling in Obergefell v. Hodges.

GBR.note: Legal in England, Wales, and Scotland since 2014; Northern Ireland since 2020.

FRA.note: Legal since 2013; France also offers civil solidarity pacts (PACS) for all couples.

DEU.note: Legal since October 2017 following a Bundestag vote.

ITA.note: Civil unions available since 2016; same-sex marriage is not recognized, and Italy restricted same-sex parents from being listed on birth certificates in 2023.

ESP.note: Legal since 2005; Spain was the third country in the world to legalize same-sex marriage.

PRT.note: Legal since 2010; adoption rights for same-sex couples followed in 2016.

NLD.note: Legal since 2001; the Netherlands was the first country in the world to legalize same-sex marriage.

BEL.note: Legal since 2003; second country in the world to legalize same-sex marriage.

SWE.note: Legal since 2009; the Church of Sweden performs religious same-sex weddings.

NOR.note: Legal since 2009; replaced the earlier registered partnership system.

DNK.note: Legal since 2012; Denmark was the first country to recognize same-sex partnerships in 1989.

FIN.note: Legal since 2017 following a citizen's initiative.

ISL.note: Legal since 2010; the law passed unanimously in parliament.

CHE.note: Legal since July 2022 following a 2021 referendum with 64% approval.

AUT.note: Legal since 2019 following a Constitutional Court ruling.

IRL.note: Legal since 2015 after a constitutional referendum with 62% approval.

POL.note: No legal recognition; the constitution defines marriage as between a man and a woman.

CZE.note: Registered partnerships available since 2006; a same-sex marriage bill has been debated but not yet passed.

HUN.note: Constitutional amendment in 2020 defined marriage as between a man and a woman; registered partnerships offer limited rights.

ROU.note: No legal recognition; a 2018 referendum to constitutionally ban same-sex marriage failed due to low turnout.

BGR.note: No legal recognition; the constitution defines marriage as a union between a man and a woman.

HRV.note: Life partnerships available since 2014; the constitution was amended in 2013 to define marriage as between a man and a woman.

GRC.note: Legal since February 2024; Greece became the first Orthodox-majority country to legalize same-sex marriage.

TUR.note: No legal recognition of same-sex relationships; homosexuality is not criminalized but societal discrimination is widespread.

RUS.note: Constitutional amendment in 2020 defined marriage as between a man and a woman; LGBTQ+ movement declared extremist in 2023.

UKR.note: No legal recognition; same-sex couples have no legal protections, though advocacy has increased during wartime.

CHN.note: No legal recognition; homosexuality was decriminalized in 1997 but same-sex relationships have no legal standing.

JPN.note: No national recognition, but many municipalities offer partnership certificates. Courts have increasingly ruled the lack of marriage equality unconstitutional.

KOR.note: No legal recognition; a 2024 Supreme Court ruling granted some insurance benefits to same-sex partners but marriage remains unavailable.

IND.note: Homosexuality decriminalized in 2018 but the Supreme Court declined to legalize same-sex marriage in 2023.

PAK.note: Homosexuality is criminalized under colonial-era laws with penalties up to life imprisonment; same-sex relationships have no recognition.

BGD.note: Homosexuality is criminalized with penalties up to life imprisonment under Section 377 of the Penal Code.

IDN.note: No legal recognition; homosexuality is criminalized in Aceh province under Sharia law and faces increasing societal hostility nationwide.

MYS.note: Homosexuality is criminalized under both civil and Sharia law with penalties up to 20 years imprisonment.

SGP.note: Section 377A was repealed in 2022 decriminalizing homosexuality, but the constitution was amended to prevent same-sex marriage.

THA.note: Legal since January 2025; Thailand became the first Southeast Asian country to legalize same-sex marriage.

VNM.note: Homosexuality is not criminalized; a 2014 law removed the ban on same-sex weddings but does not grant legal recognition.

PHL.note: No legal recognition; bills for civil partnerships have been filed but not passed.

AUS.note: Legal since December 2017 following a national postal survey with 61.6% approval.

NZL.note: Legal since 2013; New Zealand was the first country in the Asia-Pacific to legalize.

BRA.note: Legal since 2013 following a Supreme Court ruling and subsequent regulation.

ARG.note: Legal since 2010; Argentina was the first Latin American country to legalize same-sex marriage.

MEX.note: Legal nationwide since 2022; previously varied by state.

COL.note: Legal since 2016 following a Constitutional Court ruling.

CHL.note: Legal since March 2022.

ZAF.note: Legal since 2006; South Africa was the first African country and fifth globally to legalize.

NGA.note: Same-sex marriage is prohibited with up to 14 years imprisonment; northern Sharia states prescribe death by stoning.

EGY.note: No explicit anti-homosexuality law but same-sex conduct is prosecuted under debauchery and morality laws.

SAU.note: Homosexuality is punishable by death under Sharia law; no recognition of same-sex relationships.

ARE.note: Homosexuality is criminalized with potential death penalty under federal law; no recognition of same-sex relationships.

ISR.note: No same-sex marriage but marriages performed abroad are recognized; unregistered cohabitation grants significant rights.

IRN.note: Homosexuality is punishable by death under Sharia law.

IRQ.note: A 2024 law criminalized same-sex relationships with up to 15 years imprisonment.

KEN.note: Homosexuality is criminalized with up to 14 years imprisonment; a 2019 court ruling upheld the colonial-era laws.

ETH.note: Homosexuality is criminalized with up to 15 years imprisonment.

MAR.note: Homosexuality is criminalized with 6 months to 3 years imprisonment.

TWN.note: Legal since May 2019; Taiwan was the first country in Asia to legalize same-sex marriage.

EST.note: Legal since January 2024; the first former Soviet state to legalize same-sex marriage.

SVN.note: Legal since July 2023 following a Constitutional Court ruling.

topics.name: Gun Ownership
topics.description: Civilian firearm ownership regulations — from constitutional right to total prohibition.

CAN.note: Firearms require a license; handguns were frozen for sale/transfer in 2022. Long guns are available with a Possession and Acquisition License.

USA.note: Constitutional right under the Second Amendment; regulations vary by state. Highest civilian gun ownership rate globally at 120.5 per 100 people.

GBR.note: Handguns are banned since 1997; shotguns and rifles require police-issued certificates with strict conditions.

FRA.note: Firearms are categorized into classes; most require a license, background checks, and a medical exam. Handguns require sport shooting club membership.

DEU.note: Strict licensing system requiring demonstrated need, background checks, and regular inspections. Hunters and sport shooters can obtain licenses.

ITA.note: License required with background checks, mental health evaluation, and demonstrated purpose (hunting, sport, or collection).

ESP.note: Strict licensing; firearms restricted to hunting, sport shooting, and security. License renewal every 5 years with medical and psychological tests.

PRT.note: License required with background checks; self-defense is an accepted reason but requires additional justification.

NLD.note: Very restrictive; firearms only for sport shooting or hunting with strict licensing, and handguns only with sport shooting association membership.

BEL.note: License required for all firearms; hunting and sport shooting are accepted purposes. Self-defense is difficult to justify.

SWE.note: License required for each firearm; hunting and sport shooting are the main accepted purposes. One of Europe's higher ownership rates.

NOR.note: License required; semi-automatic weapons are banned since 2021. Hunting and sport shooting are accepted reasons.

DNK.note: Strict licensing; firearms mainly for hunting and sport shooting. Police approval required.

FIN.note: License required with 2-year probation for first-time owners; Finland has a strong hunting culture and relatively high ownership.

ISL.note: License required after medical and background checks; handguns are restricted to sport shooting.

CHE.note: Relatively permissive; a shall-issue permit system exists and many citizens retain military-issued rifles after service.

AUT.note: Shotguns and rifles available with registration; handguns require a permit. Ownership is common, especially for hunting.

IRL.note: Strict licensing for shotguns and rifles; handguns are effectively banned for civilians.

POL.note: License required with demonstrated need; sport shooting, hunting, and collection are accepted purposes.

CZE.note: Shall-issue licensing with right to carry for self-defense; one of the most liberal gun laws in Europe.

HUN.note: Very restrictive; firearms ownership requires stringent licensing and is generally limited to hunters.

ROU.note: Very restrictive; civilian ownership is limited, requiring demonstrated need and extensive background checks.

BGR.note: License required; firearms permitted for self-defense, hunting, and sport with background checks.

HRV.note: License required with background and medical checks; hunting firearms are most common.

GRC.note: License required; firearms permitted primarily for hunting and sport shooting with police approval.

TUR.note: License required; carry permits are may-issue. Turkey has significant civilian ownership despite formal restrictions.

RUS.note: Rifles and shotguns available with a license after 5 years of shotgun ownership; handguns are banned for civilians.

UKR.note: No comprehensive civilian firearms law existed before 2022; wartime distribution loosened restrictions. A new law is being drafted.

CHN.note: Civilian firearm ownership is almost entirely banned; only certain ethnic minority hunters may possess firearms.

JPN.note: Among the strictest in the world; only shotguns for hunting allowed after extensive testing. Handguns are completely banned for civilians.

KOR.note: Civilian firearms ownership is extremely restricted; guns must be stored at police stations when not in use for approved activities.

IND.note: License required under the Arms Act; permits are may-issue and difficult to obtain. Ownership is concentrated among rural landowners.

PAK.note: License required but widely available; Pakistan has very high civilian ownership, especially in tribal areas.

BGD.note: License required under the Arms Act; civilian ownership is limited and permits are difficult to obtain.

IDN.note: Civilian firearms are effectively banned; only military and police may possess guns, with narrow exceptions for certain indigenous groups.

MYS.note: Very restrictive under the Arms Act 1960; civilian ownership requires special licensing that is rarely granted.

SGP.note: Civilian firearm ownership is effectively banned under the Arms and Explosives Act.

THA.note: License required and relatively accessible; Thailand has significant civilian ownership rates.

VNM.note: Civilian firearm ownership is banned; only military and law enforcement may possess firearms.

PHL.note: License required; gun ownership is common and the Philippines has a strong gun culture.

AUS.note: Strict licensing since the 1996 National Firearms Agreement following the Port Arthur massacre; semi-automatic weapons are banned.

NZL.note: License required; semi-automatic weapons banned after the 2019 Christchurch shooting. Firearms are common among rural communities.

BRA.note: License required with minimum age of 25; regulations were loosened under Bolsonaro but tightened again under Lula.

ARG.note: License required; applicants must pass background and psychological checks. Carry permits are restricted.

MEX.note: Constitutional right exists but is heavily restricted in practice; only one legal gun store exists in the country, operated by the military.

COL.note: License required; civilian ownership is permitted with restrictions. Carry permits have been suspended multiple times.

CHL.note: License required with demonstrated need; a 2023 reform tightened regulations and increased penalties for illegal possession.

ZAF.note: License required under the Firearms Control Act 2000; self-defense is an accepted reason but the process is lengthy.

NGA.note: Heavily restricted under the Firearms Act; civilian ownership requires police licensing and most weapons are prohibited.

EGY.note: Heavily restricted; licenses are difficult to obtain and are reserved for specific professions or demonstrated need.

SAU.note: Civilian ownership requires a permit that is difficult to obtain; ownership is culturally accepted but legally controlled.

ARE.note: Very restrictive; civilian firearms require special permits from national security authorities.

ISR.note: License required with security justification; criteria were expanded after October 2023, increasing civilian ownership.

IRN.note: Civilian ownership is heavily restricted; only certain firearms for self-defense with government permission.

IRQ.note: One firearm per household is legally permitted; in practice, widespread ownership exists due to decades of conflict.

KEN.note: Very restrictive under the Firearms Act; licenses are rarely granted to civilians.

ETH.note: License required; firearms are available for self-defense but regulations have tightened amid internal conflicts.

MAR.note: Very restrictive; civilian ownership requires authorization from the Interior Ministry, rarely granted.

GTM.note: Constitutional right to bear arms; Guatemala has relatively loose regulations compared to the region.

YEM.note: Widespread civilian ownership with minimal regulation; one of the highest ownership rates globally.

PRK.note: Complete ban on civilian firearm ownership; possession is punishable by death.

ERI.note: Complete ban on civilian firearm ownership.

topics.name: Euthanasia and Assisted Suicide
topics.description: Legal status of euthanasia and assisted dying — from fully legal active euthanasia to total prohibition.

CAN.note: Medical Assistance in Dying (MAID) has been legal since 2016; expanded in 2021 to include non-terminal conditions.

USA.note: Physician-assisted suicide legal in 14 jurisdictions including Oregon, California, and New York. Active euthanasia is illegal everywhere.

GBR.note: Passive euthanasia via withdrawal of treatment is legal; assisted suicide remains illegal under the Suicide Act 1961, though a reform bill is under debate.

FRA.note: Passive euthanasia is legal under the Claeys-Leonetti law (2016) allowing deep sedation; an assisted dying bill was being debated in 2025.

DEU.note: Assisted suicide is legal following a 2020 Federal Constitutional Court ruling; active euthanasia remains illegal.

ITA.note: Passive euthanasia is permitted; Tuscany became the first region to legislate assisted suicide in 2025. Active euthanasia is illegal.

ESP.note: Both euthanasia and assisted suicide legal since June 2021 under the Organic Law on Euthanasia.

PRT.note: Law passed in 2023 legalizing euthanasia and assisted suicide for terminally ill patients; implementing regulations still being finalized.

NLD.note: Legal since 2002 under the Termination of Life on Request and Assisted Suicide Act; one of the first countries to legalize.

BEL.note: Legal since 2002; extended to minors with terminal illness in 2014, the only country with no age restriction.

SWE.note: Passive euthanasia through withdrawal of treatment is permitted; active euthanasia and assisted suicide are illegal.

NOR.note: Both euthanasia and assisted suicide are illegal; withdrawal of futile treatment is permitted.

DNK.note: Passive euthanasia through withdrawal of treatment is legal; active euthanasia and assisted suicide are illegal.

FIN.note: Both euthanasia and assisted suicide are illegal; palliative sedation is available.

ISL.note: Both euthanasia and assisted suicide are illegal.

CHE.note: Assisted suicide is legal if done without selfish motives; organizations like Dignitas and Exit facilitate the process. Active euthanasia is illegal.

AUT.note: Assisted suicide legal since 2022 for terminally ill or permanently seriously ill adults; active euthanasia remains illegal.

IRL.note: Both euthanasia and assisted suicide are illegal; advance healthcare directives are recognized.

POL.note: Both euthanasia and assisted suicide are illegal under the Penal Code.

CZE.note: Both euthanasia and assisted suicide are illegal.

HUN.note: Both euthanasia and assisted suicide are illegal; patients may refuse treatment.

ROU.note: Both euthanasia and assisted suicide are illegal under the Criminal Code.

BGR.note: Both euthanasia and assisted suicide are illegal.

HRV.note: Both euthanasia and assisted suicide are illegal.

GRC.note: Both euthanasia and assisted suicide are illegal under the Penal Code.

TUR.note: Both euthanasia and assisted suicide are illegal; considered equivalent to homicide.

RUS.note: Both euthanasia and assisted suicide are explicitly prohibited by federal law.

UKR.note: Both euthanasia and assisted suicide are illegal.

CHN.note: Both euthanasia and assisted suicide are illegal; there is no legal framework for end-of-life decisions.

JPN.note: No explicit law; court precedents allow passive euthanasia under strict conditions. Active euthanasia cases have resulted in convictions.

KOR.note: The Life-Sustaining Treatment Act (2018) allows withdrawal of futile treatment for terminal patients; active euthanasia is illegal.

IND.note: Passive euthanasia was legalized by the Supreme Court in 2018 through living wills and withdrawal of life support; active euthanasia is illegal.

PAK.note: Both euthanasia and assisted suicide are illegal under Islamic law and the Pakistan Penal Code.

BGD.note: Both euthanasia and assisted suicide are illegal.

IDN.note: Both euthanasia and assisted suicide are illegal; considered contrary to religious and cultural values.

MYS.note: Both euthanasia and assisted suicide are illegal under the Penal Code.

SGP.note: Both euthanasia and assisted suicide are illegal; advance medical directives are permitted.

THA.note: Passive euthanasia is permitted through advance directives under the National Health Act (2007); active euthanasia is illegal.

VNM.note: Both euthanasia and assisted suicide are illegal.

PHL.note: Both euthanasia and assisted suicide are illegal; influenced by strong Catholic opposition.

AUS.note: Voluntary assisted dying is legal in all six states as of 2023; each state has its own legislation with safeguards.

NZL.note: Legal since November 2021 under the End of Life Choice Act for terminally ill patients with less than 6 months to live.

BRA.note: Both euthanasia and assisted suicide are illegal; passive euthanasia through advance directives is in a legal gray area.

ARG.note: Passive euthanasia is legal through the Patient Rights Law (2012) allowing rejection of treatment; active euthanasia is illegal.

MEX.note: Advance directives and withdrawal of treatment are legal in several states; active euthanasia is illegal.

COL.note: Euthanasia was decriminalized by the Constitutional Court in 1997 and regulated in 2015; Colombia was the first Latin American country to do so.

CHL.note: Both euthanasia and assisted suicide are illegal; a bill has been proposed but not enacted.

ZAF.note: Both euthanasia and assisted suicide are illegal; court challenges have been unsuccessful.

NGA.note: Both euthanasia and assisted suicide are illegal.

EGY.note: Both euthanasia and assisted suicide are illegal under Islamic law and Egyptian law.

SAU.note: Both euthanasia and assisted suicide are strictly prohibited under Sharia law.

ARE.note: Both euthanasia and assisted suicide are illegal under Islamic law.

ISR.note: Passive euthanasia is permitted under the Dying Patient Act (2005); active euthanasia and assisted suicide are illegal.

IRN.note: Both euthanasia and assisted suicide are prohibited under Sharia law.

IRQ.note: Both euthanasia and assisted suicide are illegal.

KEN.note: Both euthanasia and assisted suicide are illegal.

ETH.note: Both euthanasia and assisted suicide are illegal under the Criminal Code.

MAR.note: Both euthanasia and assisted suicide are illegal under Moroccan law.

LUX.note: Legal since 2009; Luxembourg was the third country to legalize euthanasia.

ECU.note: Euthanasia was legalized by the Constitutional Court in 2024 for terminally ill patients.

URY.note: The Dignified Death Bill was passed in 2025, recognizing the right of terminally ill adults to request euthanasia.

topics.name: Prostitution
topics.description: Legal approaches to sex work — from full legalization and regulation to complete criminalization.

CAN.note: Selling sex is legal but buying sex, advertising, and third-party profiting are illegal under the 2014 Protection of Communities Act.

USA.note: Fully criminalized in all states except Nevada, where regulated brothels are permitted in some counties.

GBR.note: Selling and buying sex is legal but soliciting, brothel-keeping, and pimping are illegal; enforcement varies significantly.

FRA.note: Nordic model adopted in 2016; selling sex is legal but buying sex is a criminal offense punishable by fine.

DEU.note: Fully legal and regulated since 2002; sex workers must register and obtain health insurance. The Prostitution Protection Act of 2017 added further regulation.

ITA.note: Selling sex is not illegal but organized prostitution, brothels, and pimping are; enforcement is inconsistent.

ESP.note: Neither legal nor illegal; exists in a legal gray area. Pimping and trafficking are criminal offenses.

PRT.note: Selling sex is not explicitly illegal but pimping and brothel-keeping are; sex workers lack legal protections.

NLD.note: Fully legal and regulated since 2000; brothels operate with municipal licenses and sex workers have labor rights.

BEL.note: Decriminalized since June 2022; Belgium became the first European country to fully decriminalize sex work with labor protections.

SWE.note: Pioneer of the Nordic model since 1999; buying sex is illegal while selling is not. Aimed at reducing demand.

NOR.note: Adopted the Nordic model in 2009; buying sex is illegal with fines or imprisonment.

DNK.note: Selling sex is legal and not regulated as a profession; buying sex is legal. Pimping is illegal.

FIN.note: Selling sex is legal but buying sex from a trafficking victim is illegal; organized prostitution is prohibited.

ISL.note: Adopted the Nordic model in 2009; buying sex is illegal.

CHE.note: Fully legal and regulated; sex workers must register in most cantons. Switzerland has had legal prostitution since 1942.

AUT.note: Legal and regulated at the state level; sex workers must register, undergo health checks, and pay taxes.

IRL.note: Adopted the Nordic model in 2017; buying sex is a criminal offense.

POL.note: Selling sex is legal but organized prostitution, pimping, and brothels are illegal.

CZE.note: Exists in a legal gray area; not explicitly illegal but not regulated. Pimping and trafficking are criminal offenses.

HUN.note: Legal if practiced in designated tolerance zones; sex workers must register and undergo regular health checks.

ROU.note: Fully criminalized; both selling and buying sex are illegal.

BGR.note: Not explicitly illegal but pimping and brothels are prohibited; effectively unregulated.

HRV.note: Fully criminalized; both prostitution and solicitation are illegal.

GRC.note: Legal and regulated; sex workers must register, obtain a permit, and undergo regular health checks. In practice, most work informally.

TUR.note: Legal and regulated in licensed brothels; sex workers must register and have regular health examinations.

RUS.note: Illegal; penalties include fines for sex workers and imprisonment for organizers.

UKR.note: Illegal; both selling and buying sex are criminal offenses with fines or administrative penalties.

CHN.note: Illegal; penalties include fines, detention, and re-education. Periodic crackdowns target both sex workers and clients.

JPN.note: Prostitution is technically illegal under the Anti-Prostitution Law of 1956 but enforcement is limited; various 'adult entertainment' services operate in gray areas.

KOR.note: Fully criminalized since 2004; both buying and selling sex are illegal with imprisonment up to 1 year.

IND.note: Sex work itself is not illegal, but soliciting, running a brothel, and pimping are; enforcement varies widely.

PAK.note: Fully illegal under the Pakistan Penal Code and considered a moral offense.

BGD.note: Legal; Bangladesh is one of the few Muslim-majority countries where sex work is not criminalized, with registered brothels operating.

IDN.note: Illegal under regional regulations; the government has closed most red-light districts since 2014.

MYS.note: Fully illegal under both civil and Sharia law.

SGP.note: Selling sex is not illegal but soliciting, living off earnings, and maintaining a brothel are; Geylang is a known tolerance zone.

THA.note: Technically illegal under the Prevention and Suppression of Prostitution Act (1996) but widely tolerated and a major industry.

VNM.note: Illegal; sex workers face administrative fines and can be sent to rehabilitation centers.

PHL.note: Fully criminalized under the Revised Penal Code.

AUS.note: Legal and regulated in most states and territories; New South Wales fully decriminalized in 1995.

NZL.note: Fully decriminalized since 2003 under the Prostitution Reform Act; widely considered the global gold standard.

BRA.note: Selling sex is legal and not a criminal offense; pimping and operating brothels are illegal.

ARG.note: Selling sex is not criminalized at the federal level but local laws vary; pimping and trafficking are illegal.

MEX.note: Legal at the federal level; regulated in some states with health card requirements. Pimping is illegal.

COL.note: Legal; sex workers have labor rights recognized by the Constitutional Court. Tolerance zones exist in major cities.

CHL.note: Neither explicitly legal nor illegal; exists in a legal gray area. Pimping and trafficking are criminal offenses.

ZAF.note: Fully criminalized under the Sexual Offences Act; reform has been debated but not enacted.

NGA.note: Illegal in most of the country; northern Sharia states impose severe penalties.

EGY.note: Fully illegal under Egyptian law; penalties include imprisonment.

SAU.note: Strictly prohibited under Sharia law; severe penalties including imprisonment and corporal punishment.

ARE.note: Illegal under both civil and Sharia law; penalties include imprisonment and deportation for foreign nationals.

ISR.note: Nordic model adopted in 2020; buying sex became a criminal offense while selling remains legal.

IRN.note: Strictly prohibited under Sharia law; severe penalties including imprisonment and flogging.

IRQ.note: Illegal; penalties include imprisonment.

KEN.note: Illegal under the Penal Code; enforcement is inconsistent.

ETH.note: Selling sex is not explicitly illegal but pimping and soliciting are; sex work is widespread but unregulated.

MAR.note: Illegal under the Penal Code; penalties include imprisonment of 6 months to 2 years.

ECU.note: Legal; sex workers are registered and must undergo regular health checks.

PER.note: Legal for adults; sex workers must register and carry health cards.

topics.name: Gambling
topics.description: Legal status of gambling activities — from broadly permitted to fully banned.

CAN.note: Legal; regulated by provincial governments. Online gambling was legalized in Ontario in 2022.

USA.note: Legal but varies widely by state; sports betting has expanded rapidly since the 2018 PASPA ruling. Online gambling is legal in some states.

GBR.note: Broadly legal and well-regulated under the Gambling Act 2005; the UK Gambling Commission oversees all gambling activities.

FRA.note: Legal with restrictions; online sports betting and poker are regulated by the ANJ. Online casinos are prohibited.

DEU.note: Legal under the Interstate Treaty on Gambling (2021); online casinos, poker, and sports betting are regulated at the state level.

ITA.note: Legal and regulated by the Agenzia delle Dogane e dei Monopoli; both land-based and online gambling are permitted.

ESP.note: Legal and regulated by the DGOJ; online gambling is permitted with strict advertising restrictions introduced in 2021.

PRT.note: Legal and regulated since 2015 for online gambling; the SRIJ oversees licensing.

NLD.note: Legal; online gambling was legalized in 2021 under the Remote Gambling Act regulated by the Kansspelautoriteit.

BEL.note: Legal and regulated by the Belgian Gaming Commission; strict advertising rules and player protection measures.

SWE.note: Legal and regulated since 2019 under a licensing system; Spelinspektionen oversees the market.

NOR.note: State monopoly through Norsk Tipping and Norsk Rikstoto; private and international operators are not licensed.

DNK.note: Legal and regulated; Danske Spil holds the monopoly on lotteries while online betting and casinos are licensed.

FIN.note: State monopoly through Veikkaus; private gambling operators are not licensed.

ISL.note: Limited legal gambling; the University of Iceland Lottery and a few state-sanctioned games exist. Online gambling is unregulated.

CHE.note: Legal and regulated under the 2019 Gambling Act; online gambling is restricted to Swiss-licensed casinos.

AUT.note: Legal; casinos and online gambling are regulated under the Gambling Act. Casinos Austria holds the main license.

IRL.note: Legal; the Gambling Regulation Bill is modernizing the regulatory framework. Betting shops and online gambling are widespread.

POL.note: Legal with restrictions; online gambling is limited to a state monopoly, and only state-owned Totalizator Sportowy can operate.

CZE.note: Legal and regulated; both land-based and online gambling are permitted under the 2017 Gambling Act.

HUN.note: Legal under state supervision; Szerencsejatek operates the state lottery. Online gambling by foreign operators is blocked.

ROU.note: Legal and regulated by the ONJN; both land-based and online gambling are permitted.

BGR.note: Legal and regulated under the Gambling Act; both land-based and online gambling are permitted.

HRV.note: Legal and regulated; casinos, betting shops, and online gambling are permitted with licensing.

GRC.note: Legal and regulated by the Hellenic Gaming Commission; online gambling was formally regulated in 2020.

TUR.note: Most forms of gambling are prohibited; only state-run sports betting (Iddaa) and horse racing are legal. Online gambling is banned.

RUS.note: Gambling is restricted to four designated gambling zones; online gambling is largely prohibited except state-run sports betting.

UKR.note: Legalized in 2020 after years of prohibition; the Commission for the Regulation of Gambling and Lotteries oversees the market.

CHN.note: Almost entirely banned on the mainland; only state-run lotteries are legal. Macau is the sole exception with licensed casinos.

JPN.note: Most gambling is illegal but exceptions exist for horse racing, cycling, motorsports, and boat racing. Pachinko operates in a legal gray area. IR casino law passed in 2018.

KOR.note: Strictly regulated; only one casino (Kangwon Land) is open to Korean citizens. Foreigners-only casinos exist in several cities.

IND.note: Varies by state; Goa and Sikkim have legal casinos. Online gambling regulation is fragmented. A sweeping ban on online gambling was issued in 2025.

PAK.note: Fully banned under the Prevention of Gambling Act and Islamic law.

BGD.note: Mostly prohibited; horse racing is the only legal form of gambling. Online gambling is banned.

IDN.note: Fully banned under both civil law and Sharia principles; penalties include imprisonment.

MYS.note: Legal for non-Muslims with licensing; Genting Highlands operates the only legal casino. Gambling is haram and prohibited for Muslims.

SGP.note: Legal and regulated; two integrated resorts (Marina Bay Sands and Resorts World Sentosa) operate with strict oversight.

THA.note: Mostly illegal under the Gambling Act 1935; only the state lottery and horse racing are permitted. A casino legalization bill was approved in 2024.

VNM.note: Mostly prohibited; a few licensed casinos cater primarily to foreigners. Vietnamese citizens can enter only one casino under a pilot program.

PHL.note: Legal and regulated by PAGCOR; casinos, online gambling, and sports betting are widely available.

AUS.note: Broadly legal and regulated at the state/territory level; sports betting and casinos are widespread. Online casinos are banned for operators.

NZL.note: Legal and regulated under the Gambling Act 2003; up to 15 online casino licenses planned by 2026.

BRA.note: Sports betting was legalized in 2024; casinos and bingo halls remain illegal under a 1946 law, though reform is debated.

ARG.note: Legal and regulated at the provincial level; both land-based and online gambling are widely available.

MEX.note: Legal with licensing; casinos and sports betting are regulated under the Federal Gaming and Raffles Law.

COL.note: Legal and regulated by Coljuegos; online gambling was regulated in 2016.

CHL.note: Legal with state oversight; six casinos operate under licenses. Online gambling regulation is pending.

ZAF.note: Legal and regulated under the National Gambling Act; 41 licensed casinos operate. Online gambling is being regulated.

NGA.note: Legal and regulated by the National Lottery Regulatory Commission; sports betting is extremely popular.

EGY.note: Mostly prohibited for Egyptian Muslims; some casinos operate catering to tourists and non-Muslims.

SAU.note: Fully banned under Sharia law; all forms of gambling are prohibited.

ARE.note: Gambling is prohibited under federal law; however, Ras Al Khaimah announced plans for an integrated resort.

ISR.note: Mostly prohibited; only the state-run Mifal HaPayis lottery and Toto sports betting are legal.

IRN.note: Fully banned under Islamic law; all forms of gambling are prohibited.

IRQ.note: Prohibited under Iraqi law; gambling is considered haram.

KEN.note: Legal and regulated by the Betting Control and Licensing Board; sports betting is extremely popular.

ETH.note: Legal with restrictions; the National Lottery Administration oversees legal gambling. Betting shops are common.

MAR.note: Legal and regulated; MDJS operates sports betting and the national lottery. Casinos are open to tourists and non-Muslim locals.

MCO.note: Broadly legal; Monte Carlo is one of the world's most famous casino destinations. Monegasque citizens are banned from gambling in local casinos.

AFG.note: Fully banned under Taliban rule and Islamic law.

QAT.note: Fully banned under Islamic law; all forms of gambling are prohibited.

topics.name: Age of Criminal Responsibility
topics.description: Minimum age at which a child can be held criminally responsible — varying from under 10 to 16 and above.

CAN.note: Age 12 under the Youth Criminal Justice Act. Below 12, children cannot be charged with criminal offenses.

USA.note: Varies by state from 6 to 12; some states have no minimum age. Federal minimum is determined case-by-case.

GBR.note: Age 10 in England, Wales, and Northern Ireland; age 12 in Scotland (raised from 8 in 2019).

FRA.note: Age 13 for criminal penalties; children aged 10-12 can face educational measures.

DEU.note: Age 14 under the Youth Courts Act; below 14, children are considered non-culpable.

ITA.note: Age 14 under the Italian Penal Code; children under 14 are presumed incapable of criminal intent.

ESP.note: Age 14 under the Organic Law on Criminal Responsibility of Minors.

PRT.note: Age 16 for criminal responsibility; children aged 12-15 can face educational guardianship measures.

NLD.note: Age 12 under the Dutch Penal Code; juvenile criminal law applies from 12 to 17.

BEL.note: Age 12 under the Youth Protection Act; below 12 only protective measures apply.

SWE.note: Age 15; children under 15 cannot be prosecuted, though social services may intervene.

NOR.note: Age 15 under the Norwegian Penal Code.

DNK.note: Age 15; reduced from 15 to 14 briefly in 2010 but raised back to 15 in 2012.

FIN.note: Age 15 under the Finnish Penal Code; children under 15 may be liable for damages.

ISL.note: Age 15 under the Icelandic Penal Code.

CHE.note: Age 10 under the Juvenile Criminal Law Act; penalties differ significantly from adult criminal law.

AUT.note: Age 14 under the Austrian Penal Code.

IRL.note: Age 12 for most offenses; age 10 for murder, manslaughter, rape, and aggravated sexual assault.

POL.note: Age 15 for serious offenses; age 17 for general criminal responsibility. Children 13+ may face family court proceedings.

CZE.note: Age 15 under the Czech Criminal Code.

HUN.note: Age 14; lowered to 12 for certain violent crimes (murder, robbery, assault) since 2013.

ROU.note: Age 14 under the Romanian Penal Code; discernment must be proven for ages 14-16.

BGR.note: Age 14 under the Bulgarian Penal Code.

HRV.note: Age 14 under Croatian law; juvenile sanctions differ from adult penalties.

GRC.note: Age 15 for criminal penalties; children aged 12-14 may face reformatory or therapeutic measures.

TUR.note: Age 12 under the Turkish Penal Code; reduced penalties for ages 12-15.

RUS.note: Age 16 generally; age 14 for serious crimes such as murder, robbery, and terrorism.

UKR.note: Age 16 generally; age 14 for serious crimes including murder, robbery, and rape.

CHN.note: Age 12 for certain serious crimes (since 2021 amendment); age 14 for most crimes; age 16 for general criminal responsibility.

JPN.note: Age 14 under the Penal Code; the Juvenile Act applies to offenders under 20 (lowered to 18 for some purposes in 2022).

KOR.note: Age 14 under the Criminal Act; juveniles aged 10-13 can be subject to protective dispositions.

IND.note: Age 7 under the Indian Penal Code (Section 82); children aged 7-12 may be exempt if lacking maturity to understand consequences.

PAK.note: Age 7 under the Pakistan Penal Code; similar to Indian law with discretion for ages 7-12.

BGD.note: Age 9 under the Bangladesh Penal Code (Section 82).

IDN.note: Age 12 under the Juvenile Justice System Act of 2012.

MYS.note: Age 10 under Section 82 of the Penal Code; children aged 10-12 may be exempt based on maturity.

SGP.note: Age 10 under Section 82 of the Penal Code; similar to Malaysian law.

THA.note: Age 10 for criminal liability; children under 10 cannot be held responsible. Full criminal responsibility at 15.

VNM.note: Age 14 for very serious crimes; age 16 for general criminal responsibility.

PHL.note: Age 15 under the Juvenile Justice and Welfare Act of 2006; raised from 9.

AUS.note: Age 10 in most states (doli incapax applies for 10-13); the ACT raised it to 12 in 2024, with campaigns to raise it nationally to 14.

NZL.note: Age 10 for murder and manslaughter; age 14 for other offenses.

BRA.note: Age 12 for juvenile measures under the Child and Adolescent Statute; full criminal responsibility at 18.

ARG.note: Age 16 for criminal prosecution; children aged 16-18 are subject to a special juvenile regime.

MEX.note: Age 12 for juvenile justice proceedings under the National Law on Integral Justice for Adolescents.

COL.note: Age 14 under the Code of Childhood and Adolescence.

CHL.note: Age 14 under the Adolescent Criminal Responsibility Law.

ZAF.note: Age 12 under the Child Justice Act of 2008; raised from 10.

NGA.note: Age 7 under the Criminal Code; northern states may set different ages under Sharia law.

EGY.note: Age 12 under the Child Law; children below 12 cannot be held criminally responsible.

SAU.note: No fixed minimum age in law; judges exercise discretion based on puberty and Islamic jurisprudence.

ARE.note: Age 7; children aged 7-11 may face rehabilitation measures rather than criminal penalties.

ISR.note: Age 12 under Israeli law.

IRN.note: Age 9 for girls and 15 for boys under Islamic law, based on the age of puberty.

IRQ.note: Age 9 under the Iraqi Penal Code.

KEN.note: Age 8 under the Children Act.

ETH.note: Age 9 under the Ethiopian Criminal Code.

MAR.note: Age 12 under the Moroccan Penal Code.

LUX.note: Age 16 for criminal prosecution; minors below 16 are subject to youth protection measures.

CUB.note: Age 16 under the Cuban Penal Code.

BRN.note: Age 7 under the Brunei Penal Code.

topics.name: Freedom of the Press
topics.description: Press freedom status based on the RSF World Press Freedom Index and national legal frameworks.

CAN.note: Strong press freedom protections; consistently ranked in the top 20 of the RSF index.

USA.note: Constitutional protections under the First Amendment but ranked 57th in 2025 due to political polarization and press hostility.

GBR.note: Generally free press with strong traditions; ranked in the top 30 despite concerns about surveillance laws and libel tourism.

FRA.note: Press freedom is constitutionally protected but ranked around 21st in 2025; concerns about police violence against journalists during protests.

DEU.note: Strong constitutional protections under the Basic Law; consistently ranked in the top 20.

ITA.note: Press is generally free but faces challenges from organized crime threats and political influence; ranked around 41st in 2025.

ESP.note: Strong press freedom; ranked in the top 20. Concerns exist about the National Security Law's potential impact.

PRT.note: Consistently ranked among the top 10 in the world for press freedom.

NLD.note: Ranked 3rd in the 2025 RSF index; strong legal protections for journalists.

BEL.note: Strong press freedom with constitutional protections; ranked in the top 15.

SWE.note: One of the world's oldest press freedom traditions with the Freedom of the Press Act dating to 1766; ranked in the top 5.

NOR.note: Ranked 1st in the 2025 RSF World Press Freedom Index.

DNK.note: Consistently ranked in the top 5 for press freedom.

FIN.note: Ranked in the top 5; strong legal protections and public media independence.

ISL.note: Ranked in the top 15; the Icelandic Modern Media Initiative aimed to make Iceland a press freedom haven.

CHE.note: Strong press freedom with constitutional protections; ranked in the top 15.

AUT.note: Generally free but concerns about political influence on public media and advertising subsidies; ranked around 28th.

IRL.note: Strong press freedom; ranked in the top 10.

POL.note: Improved after 2023 government change but concerns remain about political interference with public media; ranked around 40th.

CZE.note: Ranked 10th in the 2025 RSF index; strong press freedom after concerns during the Babis era.

HUN.note: Significant concerns about government control of media through oligarch ownership and defunding of independent outlets; ranked around 67th.

ROU.note: Press is constitutionally free but faces challenges from oligarchic ownership and political interference; ranked around 52nd.

BGR.note: Significant concerns about oligarchic media control and threats against journalists; ranked around 59th.

HRV.note: Press is generally free but faces challenges from political pressure on public media; ranked around 38th.

GRC.note: Concerns about media ownership concentration and government surveillance of journalists; ranked around 68th.

TUR.note: One of the world's largest jailers of journalists; media is heavily controlled and self-censorship is widespread. Ranked around 165th.

RUS.note: Media is heavily state-controlled; independent outlets have been shut down since 2022. Ranked around 162nd.

UKR.note: Press freedom has been impacted by the war; media consolidation occurred under martial law. Ranked around 61st.

CHN.note: Ranked 178th in 2025; the world's largest jailer of journalists with extensive censorship including the Great Firewall.

JPN.note: Press is legally free but the kisha club system limits access; self-censorship is common. Ranked around 70th.

KOR.note: Generally free but concerns about defamation laws being used against journalists; ranked around 62nd.

IND.note: Concerns about press freedom have grown under the current government; ranked around 150th with violence against journalists.

PAK.note: Journalists face significant threats including violence and censorship by military and intelligence agencies; ranked around 152nd.

BGD.note: Press freedom is restricted by the Digital Security Act; journalists face harassment and imprisonment; ranked around 165th.

IDN.note: Press is constitutionally free but faces threats in some regions, particularly Papua; ranked around 111th.

MYS.note: Improvements since 2018 but concerns remain about sedition laws and media ownership; ranked around 107th.

SGP.note: Media is tightly controlled through licensing and defamation suits; ranked around 129th.

THA.note: Lese-majeste laws severely restrict press freedom; ranked around 104th.

VNM.note: Media is state-controlled; all media outlets must be licensed by the government. Ranked around 174th.

PHL.note: Journalists face significant threats; the Philippines is one of the deadliest countries for journalists. Ranked around 134th.

AUS.note: Generally free press but concerns about national security laws and media concentration; ranked around 27th.

NZL.note: Strong press freedom; ranked in the top 15.

BRA.note: Ranked 63rd in 2025; improvements after the Bolsonaro era but threats to journalists continue, especially at local levels.

ARG.note: Generally free but concerns about media polarization and economic pressure on independent outlets; ranked around 55th.

MEX.note: Ranked 124th and the most dangerous country for journalists in the Americas; cartel violence and government complicity in attacks.

COL.note: Journalists face threats from armed groups and corruption; ranked around 104th.

CHL.note: Generally free press; ranked around 54th.

ZAF.note: Strong constitutional press freedom protections; ranked around 38th. Challenges include political pressure and media ownership concentration.

NGA.note: Journalists face harassment, detention, and violence; ranked around 112th.

EGY.note: Media is heavily controlled by the state and military; one of the world's largest jailers of journalists. Ranked around 170th.

SAU.note: Media is tightly controlled by the state; severe penalties for criticism of the government or royal family. Ranked around 166th.

ARE.note: Media operates under strict government oversight and self-censorship is widespread; ranked around 145th.

ISR.note: Generally free press but military censorship applies to security matters; ranked around 97th with significant drops during the Gaza conflict.

IRN.note: One of the most repressive countries for press freedom; journalists face imprisonment and execution. Ranked around 176th.

IRQ.note: Journalists face violence from militias and government pressure; ranked around 156th.

KEN.note: Relatively free press by regional standards but journalists face harassment; ranked around 79th.

ETH.note: Press freedom has declined due to internal conflicts; journalists face detention and communication shutdowns. Ranked around 141st.

MAR.note: Press freedom is restricted; journalists face prosecution under anti-terror and defamation laws. Ranked around 129th.

PRK.note: Ranked 179th in 2025; all media is state-owned and controlled with no independent journalism.

ERI.note: Ranked 180th (last) in the 2025 RSF index; no independent media exists and journalists have been imprisoned without trial.

EST.note: Ranked 2nd in the 2025 RSF World Press Freedom Index.

TWN.note: Strong press freedom; ranked in the top 30 and considered the freest in Asia.

topics.name: Mandatory Military Service
topics.description: Whether a country requires compulsory military service, maintains selective registration, or relies on an all-volunteer force.

CAN.note: All-volunteer force; conscription was last used in World War II.

USA.note: All-volunteer military since 1973 but males aged 18-25 must register with the Selective Service System.

GBR.note: All-volunteer force since 1963 when National Service ended.

FRA.note: Conscription suspended in 1996; a mandatory one-day Universal National Service (SNU) was introduced in 2024 for 16-year-olds.

DEU.note: Conscription suspended in 2011; a new voluntary service model is being considered amid security debates.

ITA.note: Conscription abolished in 2004; Italy maintains an all-volunteer military.

ESP.note: Conscription abolished in 2001; professional military since then.

PRT.note: Conscription abolished in 2004.

NLD.note: Conscription suspended since 1997; men still receive a registration notice but are not called up.

BEL.note: Conscription abolished in 1994; all-volunteer force.

SWE.note: Reintroduced gender-neutral conscription in 2017 after suspending it in 2010; selective call-up of about 5,000 per year.

NOR.note: Gender-neutral mandatory military service since 2015; 12 months of service with selective call-up.

DNK.note: Mandatory military service of 4-12 months for men; women can volunteer. Selected by lottery if there are more candidates than needed.

FIN.note: Mandatory military service of 6-12 months for all men; women may volunteer.

ISL.note: Has no standing military; relies on NATO and a coast guard.

CHE.note: Mandatory military service for men; approximately 18 weeks of basic training followed by annual refresher courses until age 30.

AUT.note: Mandatory 6-month military service or 9-month civilian service for men; confirmed by 2013 referendum.

IRL.note: All-volunteer Defence Forces; Ireland has a policy of military neutrality.

POL.note: Conscription suspended in 2009; a voluntary basic military training program was expanded in 2023.

CZE.note: Conscription abolished in 2004; professional military.

HUN.note: Conscription abolished in 2004; can be reinstated in wartime.

ROU.note: Conscription suspended in 2006; all-volunteer force since 2007.

BGR.note: Conscription abolished in 2008.

HRV.note: Conscription abolished in 2008.

GRC.note: Mandatory military service of 12 months for men.

TUR.note: Mandatory 6-month military service for men; a paid buyout option reduces it to 1 month.

RUS.note: Mandatory 12-month military service for men aged 18-30; partial mobilization was declared in 2022.

UKR.note: Mandatory military service; mobilization age was lowered to 25 in 2024 due to the ongoing war with Russia.

CHN.note: Technically mandatory service of 2 years but in practice selective due to large volunteer numbers.

JPN.note: All-volunteer Self-Defense Forces; Article 9 of the constitution renounces war.

KOR.note: Mandatory military service of 18-21 months for all able-bodied men.

IND.note: All-volunteer military; the Agnipath scheme introduced in 2022 recruits soldiers on 4-year contracts.

PAK.note: All-volunteer military; conscription has never been used.

BGD.note: All-volunteer armed forces.

IDN.note: All-volunteer military; conscription is authorized by law but has never been implemented.

MYS.note: National Service Training Programme (PLKN) was active 2004-2015 for selected 18-year-olds; currently suspended.

SGP.note: Mandatory 2-year National Service for all male citizens and permanent residents at age 18.

THA.note: Mandatory military service for men aged 21; a lottery system determines who serves 2 years if there are insufficient volunteers.

VNM.note: Mandatory 2-year military service for men aged 18-25.

PHL.note: All-volunteer military; the Reserved Officers Training Corps is optional in universities.

AUS.note: All-volunteer Australian Defence Force; conscription last used during the Vietnam War.

NZL.note: All-volunteer New Zealand Defence Force; conscription was last used in the 1970s.

BRA.note: Mandatory military service of 12 months for men at age 18; in practice, most are exempted.

ARG.note: Conscription abolished in 1994 after the death of a conscript sparked public outrage.

MEX.note: Mandatory 12-month national military service for all men at 18; most serve in a reduced program on weekends.

COL.note: Mandatory military service of 18 months for men; enforcement and exemptions vary.

CHL.note: Mandatory military registration for men; actual service is determined by lottery if insufficient volunteers.

ZAF.note: All-volunteer South African National Defence Force since the end of apartheid.

NGA.note: All-volunteer military; the National Youth Service Corps is a mandatory 1-year civilian program for graduates.

EGY.note: Mandatory military service of 1-3 years for men aged 18-30.

SAU.note: All-volunteer armed forces.

ARE.note: Mandatory 16-month national service for men; women may volunteer for 9 months.

ISR.note: Mandatory military service of 32 months for men and 24 months for women; one of the few countries conscripting women.

IRN.note: Mandatory 24-month military service for men; exemptions are limited.

IRQ.note: Conscription was abolished in 2003 after the fall of Saddam Hussein; all-volunteer force since.

KEN.note: All-volunteer Kenya Defence Forces.

ETH.note: All-volunteer military; emergency mobilization was used during the Tigray conflict.

MAR.note: Mandatory 12-month military service reintroduced in 2019 for men and women aged 19-25.

TWN.note: Mandatory military service extended to 12 months starting 2024, up from 4 months, due to security concerns.

BLR.note: Mandatory 18-month military service for men; 12 months for those with higher education.

topics.name: Corporal Punishment of Children
topics.description: Legal status of corporal punishment of children — from full prohibition in all settings to legal and common practice.

CAN.note: Banned in schools; Section 43 of the Criminal Code still permits 'reasonable' physical correction by parents.

USA.note: Legal in the home in all states; still permitted in public schools in 17 states, primarily in the South.

GBR.note: Banned in schools across the UK; Scotland fully banned all corporal punishment in 2020, but England still permits 'reasonable chastisement' at home.

FRA.note: Fully banned in all settings since 2019 under the 'anti-smacking' law.

DEU.note: Fully banned in all settings since 2000; children have a right to a violence-free upbringing.

ITA.note: Fully banned; the Supreme Court ruled all corporal punishment unlawful in 1996.

ESP.note: Fully banned in all settings since 2007.

PRT.note: Fully banned in all settings since 2007.

NLD.note: Fully banned in all settings since 2007.

BEL.note: Fully banned in all settings; Flanders banned it in 2022 and the federal law was updated in 2024.

SWE.note: First country in the world to ban all corporal punishment of children in 1979.

NOR.note: Fully banned in all settings since 1987.

DNK.note: Fully banned in all settings since 1997.

FIN.note: Fully banned in all settings since 1983.

ISL.note: Fully banned in all settings since 2003.

CHE.note: Banned in schools; no explicit prohibition of corporal punishment in the home, though excessive punishment is punishable.

AUT.note: Fully banned in all settings since 1989.

IRL.note: Fully banned in all settings since 2015 when the 'reasonable chastisement' defense was removed.

POL.note: Fully banned in all settings since 2010.

CZE.note: Banned in schools and childcare; no explicit full prohibition in the home.

HUN.note: Fully banned in all settings since 2004.

ROU.note: Fully banned in all settings since 2004.

BGR.note: Fully banned in all settings since 2019.

HRV.note: Fully banned in all settings since 1999.

GRC.note: Fully banned in all settings since 2006.

TUR.note: Fully banned in all settings; prohibited under the Civil Code and Child Protection Law.

RUS.note: Banned in schools; not explicitly prohibited in the home, though a 2016 law removed 'close persons' exemption for battery.

UKR.note: Fully banned in all settings since 2019.

CHN.note: Banned in schools under the Minors Protection Law; no explicit prohibition in the home, and physical discipline remains culturally accepted.

JPN.note: Fully banned in all settings since 2020 under revised Child Welfare Act and Child Abuse Prevention Act.

KOR.note: Fully banned in all settings since 2021 when the Civil Code provision permitting parental discipline was removed.

IND.note: Banned in schools by the Right to Education Act 2009; not prohibited in the home.

PAK.note: Banned in schools in some provinces but enforcement is weak; corporal punishment remains widespread in homes and schools.

BGD.note: Supreme Court ruled corporal punishment in schools unconstitutional in 2011; still lawful in the home.

IDN.note: Not explicitly prohibited in law; banned in schools by regulation but enforcement is inconsistent.

MYS.note: Legal and regulated in schools (caning for boys); also lawful in the home.

SGP.note: Legal in schools (caning for boys) and in the home; corporal punishment is also used in the criminal justice system.

THA.note: Banned in schools since 2005; not explicitly prohibited in the home.

VNM.note: Banned in schools; the Law on Children 2016 prohibits violence against children but enforcement in homes is limited.

PHL.note: Fully banned in all settings since 2022 under the Positive Discipline Act.

AUS.note: Banned in schools in all states; 'reasonable chastisement' by parents remains legal in most jurisdictions.

NZL.note: Fully banned in all settings since 2007 after a controversial referendum.

BRA.note: Fully banned in all settings since 2014 under the Menino Bernardo Law.

ARG.note: Fully banned in all settings since 2014.

MEX.note: Fully banned in all settings since 2021 following a Supreme Court ruling and legislative reforms.

COL.note: Fully banned in all settings since 2021.

CHL.note: Fully banned in all settings since 2022.

ZAF.note: Fully banned; the Constitutional Court ruled all corporal punishment unconstitutional in 2019.

NGA.note: Legal in schools and the home; widely practiced and culturally accepted across the country.

EGY.note: Banned in schools by ministerial decree but enforcement is weak; lawful in the home under 'discipline' provisions.

SAU.note: Not explicitly prohibited; corporal punishment is used in schools and homes, though recent reforms have discouraged it.

ARE.note: Banned in schools since 2008; parental corporal punishment is not explicitly prohibited.

ISR.note: Fully banned in all settings since a 2000 Supreme Court ruling.
