IRN.note: Legal in the home and schools; parental discipline rights are upheld under civil law.

IRQ.note: Not explicitly prohibited; corporal punishment is commonly practiced in homes and schools.

KEN.note: Banned in schools since 2001; not prohibited in the home under the Children Act.

ETH.note: Banned in schools but enforcement is poor; the revised Family Code does not explicitly prohibit it in the home.

MAR.note: Banned in schools by ministerial circular; not explicitly prohibited in the home.

TWN.note: Fully banned in all settings; schools banned it in 2006 and home prohibition was clarified in law.

BLR.note: Banned in schools; not explicitly prohibited in the home.

topics.name: Blasphemy Laws
topics.description: Whether a country criminalizes blasphemy, religious insult, or defamation of religion — from no such laws to death penalty.

CAN.note: Blasphemous libel was repealed from the Criminal Code in 2018.

USA.note: No federal blasphemy law; the First Amendment protects freedom of speech and religion. Remaining state laws are unenforceable.

GBR.note: Blasphemy laws were abolished in England and Wales in 2008; Scotland abolished them in 2021.

FRA.note: No blasphemy law since 1881; France strongly defends secularism (laicite) and free expression.

DEU.note: Section 166 of the Criminal Code criminalizes defamation of religious groups if it could disturb public peace; rarely enforced.

ITA.note: Blasphemy was decriminalized in 1999 and reduced to an administrative fine; rarely enforced.

ESP.note: Article 525 of the Penal Code criminalizes offending religious feelings; penalties include fines. Rarely prosecuted.

PRT.note: Blasphemy laws were repealed in 2007.

NLD.note: Blasphemy law was repealed in 2014.

BEL.note: Blasphemy laws were abolished in 2023.

SWE.note: No blasphemy law; Sweden has strong free speech protections.

NOR.note: Blasphemy law was repealed in 2015.

DNK.note: Blasphemy law was repealed in 2017.

FIN.note: Section 10 of the Criminal Code criminalizes blasphemy as breach of sanctity of religion; prison up to 6 months.

ISL.note: Blasphemy law was repealed in 2015.

CHE.note: No specific blasphemy law; general hate speech provisions may apply.

AUT.note: Section 188 criminalizes disparagement of religious doctrines; penalties include up to 6 months imprisonment.

IRL.note: Constitutional blasphemy provision removed by referendum in 2018; the 2020 bill formally repealed the offense.

POL.note: Article 196 criminalizes offending religious feelings with up to 2 years imprisonment; actively prosecuted.

CZE.note: No blasphemy law; strong secular traditions.

HUN.note: No specific blasphemy law.

ROU.note: No specific blasphemy law.

BGR.note: No specific blasphemy law.

HRV.note: No specific blasphemy law.

GRC.note: Articles 198-199 of the Penal Code criminalize blasphemy and religious insult; occasionally enforced.

TUR.note: Article 216 of the Penal Code criminalizes denigrating religious values; prosecutions have increased.

RUS.note: 2013 law criminalizes offending religious feelings with up to 3 years imprisonment; used to prosecute critics.

UKR.note: No specific blasphemy law.

CHN.note: No specific blasphemy law, though religious expression is controlled through other regulations.

JPN.note: No blasphemy law; Japan has strong secular legal traditions.

KOR.note: No blasphemy law.

IND.note: Section 295A of the IPC criminalizes deliberate acts intended to outrage religious feelings with up to 3 years imprisonment; frequently used.

PAK.note: Among the world's strictest blasphemy laws; Sections 295-298 prescribe penalties up to death for defaming the Prophet Muhammad.

BGD.note: Section 295A of the Penal Code criminalizes hurting religious sentiments; blasphemy accusations have led to mob violence.

IDN.note: Blasphemy law (Article 156a) carries up to 5 years imprisonment; actively enforced, including against the former Jakarta governor in 2017.

MYS.note: Blasphemy is criminalized under both federal and state Sharia laws; penalties vary by state.

SGP.note: Sections 298-298A of the Penal Code criminalize wounding religious feelings with up to 3 years imprisonment.

THA.note: No specific blasphemy law, though the lese-majeste law protects the monarchy rather than religion.

VNM.note: No specific blasphemy law; the state controls religious activities through registration requirements.

PHL.note: No blasphemy law; the constitution protects religious freedom and free expression.

AUS.note: No federal blasphemy law; state-level laws have been repealed or are considered obsolete.

NZL.note: Blasphemy law was repealed in 2019.

BRA.note: No blasphemy law; the constitution guarantees freedom of religion and expression.

ARG.note: No specific blasphemy law.

MEX.note: No blasphemy law; Mexico is a secular state.

COL.note: No blasphemy law.

CHL.note: No blasphemy law.

ZAF.note: No blasphemy law; the constitution protects free expression.

NGA.note: Blasphemy is criminalized in northern Sharia states with severe penalties including death; federal law also restricts insults to religion.

EGY.note: Article 98(f) of the Penal Code criminalizes contempt of religion with up to 5 years imprisonment; frequently used against minorities.

SAU.note: Blasphemy is punishable by death under Sharia law; the anti-cybercrime law is also used to prosecute online blasphemy.

ARE.note: Blasphemy is criminalized under the Penal Code and anti-discrimination law with penalties including imprisonment.

ISR.note: British-era blasphemy provisions remain in law but are virtually never enforced.

IRN.note: Blasphemy against Islam or the Prophet is punishable by death; apostasy also carries the death penalty.

IRQ.note: Article 372 of the Penal Code criminalizes insulting religious practices with up to 3 years imprisonment.

KEN.note: Section 138 of the Penal Code technically criminalizes insulting religion but is rarely enforced.

ETH.note: Article 492 of the Criminal Code criminalizes outraging religious feelings but is seldom enforced.

MAR.note: Article 220 of the Penal Code criminalizes undermining the Islamic faith with up to 3 years imprisonment.

TWN.note: No blasphemy law; strong free expression protections.

BLR.note: Article 130 of the Criminal Code covers incitement of religious hatred but there is no specific blasphemy law; rarely used for blasphemy.

topics.name: Internet Censorship
topics.description: Extent of government control over internet content — from free and open access to pervasive censorship.

CAN.note: Free and open internet; no government censorship of online content.

USA.note: Broadly free internet protected by the First Amendment; limited restrictions on specific illegal content.

GBR.note: Generally free but the Online Safety Act 2023 requires platforms to remove illegal content; ISP-level blocks on some categories.

FRA.note: Generally free; the Avia Law and subsequent regulations require platforms to remove hate speech and terrorist content.

DEU.note: Generally free; the NetzDG law requires removal of illegal content including hate speech within 24 hours.

ITA.note: Generally free and open internet with limited restrictions.

ESP.note: Free and open internet; limited restrictions on specific illegal content.

PRT.note: Free and open internet; among the least restricted in Europe.

NLD.note: Free and open internet; net neutrality is legally protected.

BEL.note: Free and open internet.

SWE.note: Free and open internet; strong digital rights protections.

NOR.note: Free and open internet; voluntary ISP-level blocking of child abuse material.

DNK.note: Free and open internet with minimal restrictions.

FIN.note: Free and open internet; censorship by the government is prohibited.

ISL.note: Free and open internet; Iceland aims to be an internet freedom haven.

CHE.note: Free and open internet; limited blocking of unlicensed gambling sites.

AUT.note: Free and open internet.

IRL.note: Free and open internet.

POL.note: Generally free internet; limited blocking of gambling and some illegal content.

CZE.note: Free and open internet.

HUN.note: Generally accessible but concerns about government surveillance and media influence extending to online spaces.

ROU.note: Free and open internet; Romania has some of the fastest internet speeds in Europe.

BGR.note: Generally free internet.

HRV.note: Free and open internet.

GRC.note: Generally free internet; concerns about surveillance under national security frameworks.

TUR.note: Significant censorship; over 400,000 websites blocked including Wikipedia (unblocked 2020). Social media is frequently throttled during crises.

RUS.note: Pervasive censorship under the 'sovereign internet' law; VPNs are restricted, and many platforms including Meta are banned.

UKR.note: Russian platforms and media are banned for national security; otherwise generally open.

CHN.note: The Great Firewall blocks Google, Facebook, Twitter/X, Wikipedia, and thousands of foreign sites; extensive domestic content moderation.

JPN.note: Free and open internet; self-regulation by industry with limited government intervention.

KOR.note: Generally free but the Korea Communications Standards Commission blocks North Korean content and some adult material.

IND.note: Increasing internet shutdowns (more than any country); content blocking under IT Act; frequent platform restrictions in Kashmir.

PAK.note: Significant censorship; social media platforms are periodically blocked, and the Pakistan Telecommunication Authority filters content.

BGD.note: Increasing restrictions; social media has been blocked during protests, and the Digital Security Act criminalizes online speech.

IDN.note: Government requires platform registration under MR5 regulation; blocks content related to LGBTQ+, gambling, and political criticism.

MYS.note: Generally accessible but the Communications and Multimedia Commission increasingly blocks political content and news sites.

SGP.note: Moderate filtering; the POFMA law targets 'false statements' and the government can order content corrections or takedowns.

THA.note: Extensive blocking of lese-majeste content; the Computer Crime Act is used to censor political speech and criticism.

VNM.note: Extensive censorship; the Cybersecurity Law requires data localization and content removal. Bloggers are frequently imprisoned.

PHL.note: Generally accessible but the Cybercrime Prevention Act raises concerns about online libel provisions.

AUS.note: Generally free; the eSafety Commissioner can order content removal, and social media for under-16s was banned in 2024.

NZL.note: Free and open internet; limited restrictions on objectionable content.

BRA.note: Generally free under the Marco Civil da Internet; courts have ordered temporary platform blocks including X/Twitter in 2024.

ARG.note: Free and open internet.

MEX.note: Generally free internet; net neutrality is protected by law.

COL.note: Free and open internet.

CHL.note: Free and open internet; net neutrality is legally protected.

ZAF.note: Generally free internet.

NGA.note: Generally accessible but the government suspended Twitter for 7 months in 2021; increasing regulation of social media.

EGY.note: Extensive blocking of news sites and VPNs; the anti-cybercrime law criminalizes online dissent.

SAU.note: Significant filtering of political, religious, and social content; VoIP services were unblocked in 2017.

ARE.note: Substantial filtering of political content, VoIP services, and LGBTQ+ content; cybercrime law criminalizes online criticism.

ISR.note: Generally free internet; some content removal orders related to national security.

IRN.note: Pervasive censorship; most social media platforms are blocked, and the National Information Network aims to create a controlled domestic internet.

IRQ.note: Frequent internet shutdowns during protests; social media is periodically blocked.

KEN.note: Generally free internet; some concerns about the Computer Misuse and Cybercrimes Act.

ETH.note: Frequent internet shutdowns during conflicts; social media blocked during political unrest. One of the highest shutdown counts globally.

MAR.note: Generally accessible but VoIP services are blocked; some news sites are restricted.

TWN.note: Free and open internet; Taiwan is among the freest in Asia for internet access.

BLR.note: Extensive censorship since 2020 protests; independent news sites blocked, VPNs restricted, and internet shutdowns during political events.

topics.name: Data Privacy Protection
topics.description: Strength of personal data protection laws — from comprehensive GDPR-level frameworks to minimal or no legal protection.

CAN.note: PIPEDA governs private-sector data; considered adequate by the EU. Provincial laws in Quebec, Alberta, and BC add protections.

USA.note: No comprehensive federal privacy law; sectoral laws like HIPAA and COPPA cover specific areas. State laws like CCPA/CPRA provide broader protection.

GBR.note: UK GDPR and Data Protection Act 2018 provide comprehensive protection; post-Brexit reforms maintain high standards.

FRA.note: GDPR applies; CNIL is among the most active data protection authorities in Europe.

DEU.note: GDPR applies; Germany has some of the strongest privacy traditions, with the BDSG supplementing EU rules.

ITA.note: GDPR applies; the Garante per la protezione dei dati personali actively enforces data protection.

ESP.note: GDPR applies; supplemented by the Organic Law on Data Protection and Digital Rights (LOPDGDD).

PRT.note: GDPR applies; the CNPD oversees data protection compliance.

NLD.note: GDPR applies; the Dutch DPA (Autoriteit Persoonsgegevens) is active in enforcement.

BEL.note: GDPR applies; home of the European Data Protection Board.

SWE.note: GDPR applies; Sweden has had data protection law since 1973.

NOR.note: GDPR applies through the EEA Agreement; the Norwegian DPA (Datatilsynet) actively enforces.

DNK.note: GDPR applies; the Danish DPA oversees compliance.

FIN.note: GDPR applies; supplemented by the Finnish Data Protection Act.

ISL.note: GDPR applies through the EEA Agreement.

CHE.note: New Federal Act on Data Protection (nFADP) took effect in 2023, aligning closely with GDPR; recognized as adequate by the EU.

AUT.note: GDPR applies; the Austrian DPA enforces data protection.

IRL.note: GDPR applies; the Data Protection Commission oversees many major tech companies headquartered in Ireland.

POL.note: GDPR applies; the Personal Data Protection Office (UODO) oversees enforcement.

CZE.note: GDPR applies; supplemented by national data protection legislation.

HUN.note: GDPR applies; concerns about government surveillance practices.

ROU.note: GDPR applies; the ANSPDCP oversees enforcement.

BGR.note: GDPR applies; the Commission for Personal Data Protection oversees compliance.

HRV.note: GDPR applies as an EU member state.

GRC.note: GDPR applies; the Hellenic DPA oversees enforcement.

TUR.note: Personal Data Protection Law (KVKK) enacted in 2016; modeled on EU standards but enforcement is less robust.

RUS.note: Federal Law on Personal Data requires data localization; enforcement exists but government access to data is broad.

UKR.note: Law on Personal Data Protection exists; efforts to align with EU standards are ongoing as part of EU accession process.

CHN.note: Personal Information Protection Law (PIPL) enacted in 2021 provides comprehensive rules but the government retains broad access rights.

JPN.note: Act on Protection of Personal Information (APPI) amended in 2022; recognized as adequate by the EU.

KOR.note: Personal Information Protection Act (PIPA) is one of the strongest in Asia; recognized as adequate by the EU in 2022.

IND.note: Digital Personal Data Protection Act passed in 2023; implementation rules are still being finalized.

PAK.note: No comprehensive data protection law; the Personal Data Protection Bill remains pending.

BGD.note: No comprehensive data protection law; limited protections exist under the Digital Security Act.

IDN.note: Personal Data Protection Law enacted in 2022; a 2-year transition period with full enforcement beginning in 2024.

MYS.note: Personal Data Protection Act 2010 provides moderate protection; does not cover government agencies.

SGP.note: Personal Data Protection Act (PDPA) provides solid protection with active enforcement by the PDPC.

THA.note: Personal Data Protection Act (PDPA) fully effective since 2022; modeled partly on GDPR.

VNM.note: Personal Data Protection Decree issued in 2023; still developing enforcement capacity.

PHL.note: Data Privacy Act of 2012 provides comprehensive framework; the National Privacy Commission actively enforces.

AUS.note: Privacy Act 1988 governs data protection; a major reform bill was introduced in 2024 to strengthen protections.

NZL.note: Privacy Act 2020 modernized the framework; recognized as adequate by the EU.

BRA.note: LGPD (Lei Geral de Protecao de Dados) enacted in 2020; modeled on GDPR with the ANPD as enforcement authority.

ARG.note: Personal Data Protection Law of 2000 was the first comprehensive law in Latin America; recognized as adequate by the EU.

MEX.note: Federal Law on Protection of Personal Data Held by Private Parties provides moderate protections.

COL.note: Law 1581 of 2012 provides a comprehensive data protection framework; the SIC oversees enforcement.

CHL.note: Data protection law reformed in 2024; a new Data Protection Agency was established.

ZAF.note: Protection of Personal Information Act (POPIA) became fully enforceable in 2021.

NGA.note: Nigeria Data Protection Act signed into law in 2023; enforcement capacity is still developing.

EGY.note: Data Protection Law enacted in 2020 but implementing regulations were delayed; enforcement is nascent.

SAU.note: Personal Data Protection Law enacted in 2022; enforcement began under the Saudi Data and AI Authority (SDAIA).

ARE.note: Federal Data Protection Law enacted in 2021; free zones like DIFC and ADGM have their own comprehensive regulations.

ISR.note: Privacy Protection Act of 1981 with amendments; data protection authority has limited enforcement powers.

IRN.note: No comprehensive data protection law; the government conducts extensive surveillance.

IRQ.note: No comprehensive data protection law.

KEN.note: Data Protection Act 2019 provides a comprehensive framework modeled on GDPR; the Office of the Data Commissioner oversees enforcement.

ETH.note: No comprehensive data protection law; limited protections under the Computer Crime Proclamation.

MAR.note: Law 09-08 on Personal Data Protection enacted in 2009; the CNDP oversees compliance.

TWN.note: Personal Data Protection Act provides moderate protections; amended in 2023 to strengthen enforcement.

BLR.note: Law on Personal Data provides basic framework but government surveillance is extensive.

topics.name: Nuclear Weapons Status
topics.description: A country's relationship to nuclear weapons — from nuclear-free zones to active nuclear arsenals.

CAN.note: Non-nuclear NATO member under the US nuclear umbrella; returned US nuclear weapons stationed on its territory in the 1980s.

USA.note: Nuclear armed state with approximately 5,500 warheads; one of the five NPT-recognized nuclear weapon states.

GBR.note: Nuclear armed state with approximately 225 warheads; maintains a continuous at-sea deterrent via Trident submarines.

FRA.note: Nuclear armed state with approximately 290 warheads; maintains independent nuclear deterrent with submarines and air-launched missiles.

DEU.note: NATO nuclear sharing arrangement; US tactical nuclear weapons are stationed at Buchel Air Base.

ITA.note: NATO nuclear sharing arrangement; US tactical nuclear weapons stationed at Aviano and Ghedi air bases.

ESP.note: NPT non-nuclear state; does not participate in NATO nuclear sharing.

PRT.note: NPT non-nuclear state; NATO member without nuclear sharing.

NLD.note: NATO nuclear sharing arrangement; US tactical nuclear weapons stationed at Volkel Air Base.

BEL.note: NATO nuclear sharing arrangement; US tactical nuclear weapons stationed at Kleine Brogel Air Base.

SWE.note: NPT non-nuclear state; joined NATO in 2024 but does not host nuclear weapons.

NOR.note: NATO member but has a policy of not allowing nuclear weapons on its territory in peacetime.

DNK.note: NATO member but does not allow nuclear weapons on its territory in peacetime.

FIN.note: Joined NATO in 2023; does not host nuclear weapons.

ISL.note: NATO founding member with no military; does not host nuclear weapons.

CHE.note: Neutral non-nuclear state; not a NATO member.

AUT.note: Constitutionally neutral; signed the Treaty on Prohibition of Nuclear Weapons (TPNW).

IRL.note: Militarily neutral; signed and ratified the TPNW.

POL.note: NATO member under the nuclear umbrella; discussions about hosting US nuclear weapons have been raised.

CZE.note: NATO member; non-nuclear state without nuclear sharing.

HUN.note: NATO member; non-nuclear state.

ROU.note: NATO member; non-nuclear state.

BGR.note: NATO member; non-nuclear state.

HRV.note: NATO member; non-nuclear state.

GRC.note: NATO member; non-nuclear state. Previously hosted US nuclear weapons which were withdrawn.

TUR.note: NATO nuclear sharing arrangement; US B61 nuclear bombs are stationed at Incirlik Air Base.

RUS.note: Largest nuclear arsenal in the world with approximately 6,200 warheads; deployed tactical nuclear weapons to Belarus in 2023.

UKR.note: Gave up its inherited Soviet nuclear weapons under the 1994 Budapest Memorandum; NPT non-nuclear state.

CHN.note: Nuclear armed state with a rapidly growing arsenal estimated at over 500 warheads; NPT-recognized nuclear weapon state.

JPN.note: Non-nuclear state under the US nuclear umbrella; has the technical capability but a strong anti-nuclear policy. Only country attacked with nuclear weapons.

KOR.note: Non-nuclear state under the US nuclear umbrella; the Washington Declaration of 2023 strengthened extended deterrence.

IND.note: Nuclear armed state with approximately 170 warheads; not a signatory to the NPT.

PAK.note: Nuclear armed state with approximately 170 warheads; not a signatory to the NPT.

BGD.note: Non-nuclear state; party to the NPT and has signed the TPNW.

IDN.note: Non-nuclear state in the Southeast Asian Nuclear-Weapon-Free Zone (SEANWFZ) under the Treaty of Bangkok.

MYS.note: Non-nuclear state; member of the SEANWFZ and has ratified the TPNW.

SGP.note: Non-nuclear state; member of the SEANWFZ.

THA.note: Non-nuclear state; member of the SEANWFZ and depositary of the Treaty of Bangkok.

VNM.note: Non-nuclear state; member of the SEANWFZ.

PHL.note: Non-nuclear state; member of the SEANWFZ and ratified the TPNW.

AUS.note: Non-nuclear state under the US nuclear umbrella via ANZUS; AUKUS submarine deal does not involve nuclear weapons.

NZL.note: Nuclear-free since 1984; the Nuclear Free Zone Act bans nuclear weapons and nuclear-powered ships. Ratified the TPNW.

BRA.note: Non-nuclear state in the Latin American Nuclear-Weapon-Free Zone under the Treaty of Tlatelolco; the constitution prohibits nuclear weapons.

ARG.note: Non-nuclear state; member of the Tlatelolco Treaty zone. Abandoned its nuclear weapons program in the 1980s.

MEX.note: Non-nuclear state; initiated the Treaty of Tlatelolco establishing the first nuclear-weapon-free zone in a populated area.

COL.note: Non-nuclear state; member of the Tlatelolco Treaty zone and ratified the TPNW.

CHL.note: Non-nuclear state; member of the Tlatelolco Treaty zone.

ZAF.note: Unique case: built 6 nuclear weapons but voluntarily dismantled them by 1991; member of the African Nuclear-Weapon-Free Zone (Pelindaba Treaty).

NGA.note: Non-nuclear state; member of the African Nuclear-Weapon-Free Zone and ratified the TPNW.

EGY.note: NPT non-nuclear state; has not signed the TPNW and has pushed for a nuclear-weapon-free zone in the Middle East.

SAU.note: NPT non-nuclear state; officials have stated Saudi Arabia would seek nuclear weapons if Iran obtains them.

ARE.note: NPT non-nuclear state; operates the Barakah nuclear power plant with a commitment to non-enrichment.

ISR.note: Undeclared nuclear state with an estimated 90 warheads; maintains a policy of nuclear ambiguity. Not a signatory to the NPT.

IRN.note: NPT signatory; uranium enrichment program is a major international concern. The JCPOA was abandoned by the US in 2018.

IRQ.note: NPT non-nuclear state; nuclear program was dismantled after the 1991 Gulf War and 2003 invasion.

KEN.note: Non-nuclear state; member of the African Nuclear-Weapon-Free Zone and ratified the TPNW.

ETH.note: Non-nuclear state; member of the African Nuclear-Weapon-Free Zone.

MAR.note: Non-nuclear state; member of the African Nuclear-Weapon-Free Zone.

TWN.note: Non-nuclear under US extended deterrence; abandoned a nuclear weapons program in the 1980s under US pressure.

BLR.note: Gave up its Soviet-era nuclear weapons in the 1990s; Russia deployed tactical nuclear weapons to Belarus in 2023.

topics.name: Minimum Wage
topics.description: National statutory minimum wage levels — from high living wages to no statutory minimum.

CAN.note: Varies by province; ranges from C$15.75 to C$19.00/hr. Federal minimum is C$17.30/hr (approximately US$13).

USA.note: Federal minimum wage is $7.25/hr since 2009; many states and cities set higher rates, with some exceeding $15/hr.

GBR.note: National Living Wage is GBP 12.21/hr for workers 21+ (approximately US$15.50) as of April 2025.

FRA.note: SMIC is EUR 11.88/hr gross (approximately US$13) as of 2025; indexed to inflation.

DEU.note: EUR 12.82/hr as of 2025 (approximately US$14); introduced in 2015 and raised significantly in 2022.

ITA.note: No statutory minimum wage; wages are set through sector-level collective bargaining agreements covering most workers.

ESP.note: EUR 1,184/month for 14 payments (approximately US$8.50/hr); raised significantly in recent years.

PRT.note: EUR 870/month (approximately US$5.80/hr); has been rising rapidly as part of government policy.

NLD.note: EUR 13.68/hr as of 2025 (approximately US$15); switched from monthly to hourly minimum in 2024.

BEL.note: EUR 2,029.88/month gross (approximately US$13.50/hr); among the highest in the EU.

SWE.note: No statutory minimum wage; wages are set through collective bargaining, resulting in effective minimums of approximately SEK 140-170/hr.

NOR.note: No general statutory minimum wage; sector-specific minimums exist for some industries through collective agreements.

DNK.note: No statutory minimum wage; collective agreements cover about 80% of workers with effective minimums around DKK 130/hr (approximately US$19).

FIN.note: No statutory minimum wage; wages set through universally applicable collective agreements.

ISL.note: No statutory minimum wage; set through collective bargaining with high effective minimums.

CHE.note: No federal minimum but cantons Geneva, Neuchatel, Jura, Basel-Stadt, and Ticino have set minimums; Geneva's is CHF 24.32/hr (approximately US$27).

AUT.note: No statutory minimum wage; collective agreements set sector-specific minimums, with EUR 2,000/month as a negotiated floor.

IRL.note: EUR 13.50/hr as of 2025 (approximately US$15); on a path to becoming a living wage.

POL.note: PLN 30.50/hr as of 2025 (approximately US$7.60); has been rising sharply.

CZE.note: CZK 112.50/hr as of 2025 (approximately US$4.80); lower than Western Europe but rising.

HUN.note: HUF 290,800/month as of 2025 (approximately US$4.50/hr); higher rates for skilled workers.

ROU.note: RON 4,050/month as of 2025 (approximately US$5.00/hr); has been rising significantly.

BGR.note: BGN 1,077/month as of 2025 (approximately US$3.50/hr); the lowest in the EU but rising.

HRV.note: EUR 840/month as of 2025 (approximately US$5.60/hr).

GRC.note: EUR 880/month as of 2025 (approximately US$5.80/hr); frozen for years during the debt crisis but now rising.

TUR.note: TRY 22,104/month as of 2025 (approximately US$3.50/hr at market rates); adjusted frequently due to high inflation.

RUS.note: RUB 22,440/month as of 2025 (approximately US$2.50/hr at market rates).

UKR.note: UAH 8,000/month as of 2025 (approximately US$2.00/hr); low but rising.

CHN.note: Varies by region; Shanghai's is CNY 2,690/month (approximately US$2.30/hr). Set by local governments.

JPN.note: National weighted average of JPY 1,055/hr as of 2024 (approximately US$7); varies by prefecture, highest in Tokyo.

KOR.note: KRW 10,030/hr as of 2025 (approximately US$7.30).

IND.note: Varies widely by state and sector; national floor wage is INR 178/day (approximately US$0.25/hr). The Code on Wages aims to standardize.

PAK.note: PKR 37,000/month as of 2024 (approximately US$0.80/hr); varies by province.

BGD.note: BDT 12,500/month for garment workers as of 2024 (approximately US$0.60/hr); varies by sector.

IDN.note: Varies by province; Jakarta's is IDR 5,067,381/month (approximately US$1.90/hr).

MYS.note: MYR 1,500/month nationwide (approximately US$2.00/hr) as of 2023.

SGP.note: No general minimum wage but Progressive Wage Model sets sector-specific floors; cleaning sector starts at SGD 1,412/month (approximately US$6.20/hr).

THA.note: THB 370-400/day depending on province (approximately US$0.65-0.70/hr) as of 2025.

VNM.note: VND 4,960,000/month in Region I (approximately US$1.20/hr); varies by region.

PHL.note: Varies by region; NCR daily minimum is PHP 645 (approximately US$0.70/hr) as of 2024.

AUS.note: AUD 24.10/hr as of July 2024 (approximately US$16); one of the highest in the world.

NZL.note: NZD 23.15/hr as of April 2024 (approximately US$14).

BRA.note: BRL 1,518/month as of 2025 (approximately US$1.70/hr).

ARG.note: Frequently adjusted due to hyperinflation; effectively very low in dollar terms.

MEX.note: MXN 278.80/day as of 2025 (approximately US$1.70/hr); doubled in real terms since 2019.

COL.note: COP 1,423,500/month as of 2025 (approximately US$2.00/hr).

CHL.note: CLP 500,000/month as of 2025 (approximately US$3.20/hr).

ZAF.note: ZAR 27.58/hr as of 2024 (approximately US$1.50/hr).

NGA.note: NGN 70,000/month as of 2024 (approximately US$0.27/hr); recently raised from NGN 30,000.

EGY.note: EGP 6,000/month for private sector as of 2024 (approximately US$0.75/hr).

SAU.note: SAR 4,000/month for Saudi nationals (approximately US$6.30/hr); no minimum for foreign workers.

ARE.note: No statutory minimum wage; some sectors have proposed minimums. Wages are largely market-determined.

ISR.note: ILS 32.30/hr as of 2024 (approximately US$8.70).

IRN.note: IRR 93,140,000/month as of 2024 (approximately US$1.30/hr at market rates).

IRQ.note: IQD 350,000/month (approximately US$1.60/hr); set by the Ministry of Labor.

KEN.note: Varies by location and sector; Nairobi general minimum is KES 16,536/month (approximately US$0.75/hr).

ETH.note: No statutory minimum wage for the private sector; government employees have a minimum salary.

MAR.note: MAD 17.01/hr for non-agricultural work (approximately US$1.70/hr) as of 2025.

TWN.note: TWD 28,590/month or TWD 190/hr as of 2025 (approximately US$6/hr).

BLR.note: BYN 626/month as of 2025 (approximately US$1.50/hr at market rates).

topics.name: Voting Age
topics.description: Minimum age at which citizens can vote in national elections.

CAN.note: Voting age is 18 for federal and provincial elections.

USA.note: Voting age is 18 under the 26th Amendment (1971); some states allow 17-year-olds to vote in primaries.

GBR.note: Voting age is 18 for UK parliamentary elections; Scotland and Wales lowered it to 16 for devolved elections.

FRA.note: Voting age is 18.

DEU.note: Voting age is 18 for federal elections; lowered to 16 in some state and local elections.

ITA.note: Voting age lowered to 18 for the Senate in 2021 (previously 25); 18 for the Chamber of Deputies.

ESP.note: Voting age is 18.

PRT.note: Voting age is 18.

NLD.note: Voting age is 18.

BEL.note: Voting age is 18; lowered to 16 for European Parliament elections in 2024. Voting is compulsory.

SWE.note: Voting age is 18.

NOR.note: Voting age is 18; pilot programs have tested lowering to 16 for local elections.

DNK.note: Voting age is 18.

FIN.note: Voting age is 18.

ISL.note: Voting age is 18.

CHE.note: Voting age is 18 at the federal level; Glarus canton allows voting at 16.

AUT.note: Voting age is 16 for all elections since 2007; the first EU country to do so nationally.

IRL.note: Voting age is 18.

POL.note: Voting age is 18.

CZE.note: Voting age is 18.

HUN.note: Voting age is 18.

ROU.note: Voting age is 18.

BGR.note: Voting age is 18.

HRV.note: Voting age is 18; employed persons can vote at 16.

GRC.note: Voting age lowered to 17 in 2016.

TUR.note: Voting age is 18.

RUS.note: Voting age is 18.

UKR.note: Voting age is 18.

CHN.note: Voting age is 18 for elections to local People's Congresses; elections are not multiparty.

JPN.note: Voting age lowered from 20 to 18 in 2016.

KOR.note: Voting age lowered from 19 to 18 in 2020.

IND.note: Voting age lowered from 21 to 18 by the 61st Constitutional Amendment in 1989.

PAK.note: Voting age is 18.

BGD.note: Voting age is 18.

IDN.note: Voting age is 17 or upon marriage.

MYS.note: Voting age lowered from 21 to 18 in 2021 via the Undi18 amendment.

SGP.note: Voting age is 21; one of the few countries with such a high threshold. Voting is compulsory.

THA.note: Voting age is 18.

VNM.note: Voting age is 18.

PHL.note: Voting age is 18.

AUS.note: Voting age is 18; voting is compulsory.

NZL.note: Voting age is 18; a 2022 Supreme Court ruling found this discriminatory against 16-17 year olds but parliament has not changed the law.

BRA.note: Voting is optional at 16 and compulsory from 18 to 70.

ARG.note: Voting is optional at 16 and compulsory from 18.

MEX.note: Voting age is 18.

COL.note: Voting age is 18.

CHL.note: Voting age is 18.

ZAF.note: Voting age is 18.

NGA.note: Voting age is 18.

EGY.note: Voting age is 18.

SAU.note: Voting age is 18 for municipal elections (the only elections held); women gained the right to vote in 2015.

ARE.note: Limited electoral college system for the Federal National Council; voters are selected by the government.

ISR.note: Voting age is 18.

IRN.note: Voting age raised from 15 to 18 in 2007.

IRQ.note: Voting age is 18.

KEN.note: Voting age is 18.

ETH.note: Voting age is 18.

MAR.note: Voting age is 18.

TWN.note: Voting age lowered from 20 to 18 by constitutional amendment in 2022, but the referendum to ratify it failed.

BLR.note: Voting age is 18; elections are not considered free or fair by international observers.

topics.name: LGBTQ+ Rights
topics.description: Legal status of LGBTQ+ people — from full equality with marriage and anti-discrimination protections to criminalization with death penalty.

CAN.note: Marriage legal since 2005; comprehensive anti-discrimination protections and gender identity recognized federally.

USA.note: Marriage legal since 2015; Respect for Marriage Act signed in 2022. Anti-discrimination protections vary by state.

GBR.note: Marriage legal; Equality Act 2010 prohibits discrimination on sexual orientation and gender reassignment.

FRA.note: Marriage legal since 2013; comprehensive anti-discrimination protections.

DEU.note: Marriage legal since 2017; the General Equal Treatment Act prohibits discrimination. Self-determination gender law passed in 2024.

ITA.note: Civil unions since 2016 but no marriage equality; no comprehensive anti-discrimination law covering sexual orientation.

ESP.note: Marriage legal since 2005; comprehensive LGBTQ+ rights law passed in 2023 including self-determination for gender identity.

PRT.note: Marriage legal since 2010; gender identity law allows self-determination since 2018.

NLD.note: Marriage legal since 2001 (first in the world); comprehensive anti-discrimination protections.

BEL.note: Marriage legal since 2003; comprehensive anti-discrimination framework.

SWE.note: Marriage legal since 2009; strong anti-discrimination protections and gender identity recognition.

NOR.note: Marriage legal since 2009; comprehensive anti-discrimination protections.

DNK.note: Marriage legal since 2012; pioneered registered partnerships in 1989.

FIN.note: Marriage legal since 2017; anti-discrimination law covers sexual orientation.

ISL.note: Marriage legal since 2010; comprehensive protections and gender self-determination.

CHE.note: Marriage legal since 2022; anti-discrimination protections expanded.

AUT.note: Marriage legal since 2019; anti-discrimination protections in place.

IRL.note: Marriage legal since 2015 by popular referendum; Employment Equality Act covers sexual orientation.

POL.note: Homosexuality is legal but no recognition of same-sex relationships; some municipalities declared 'LGBT-free zones' (later rescinded under EU pressure).

CZE.note: Registered partnerships since 2006; anti-discrimination law covers sexual orientation. Marriage bill under debate.

HUN.note: Homosexuality is legal but 2020 constitutional amendment banned same-sex marriage and limited adoption; anti-LGBTQ+ content law passed in 2021.

ROU.note: Homosexuality is legal since 2001; no recognition of same-sex relationships. Limited anti-discrimination protections exist.

BGR.note: Homosexuality is legal; no recognition of same-sex relationships. The constitution defines marriage as between a man and a woman.

HRV.note: Life partnerships since 2014; anti-discrimination law covers sexual orientation.

GRC.note: Marriage legal since February 2024; first Orthodox-majority country to legalize.

TUR.note: Homosexuality is legal but no anti-discrimination protections; LGBTQ+ events are frequently banned.

RUS.note: LGBTQ+ movement declared extremist in 2023; 'propaganda' law expanded in 2022 to ban all positive depictions.

UKR.note: Homosexuality is legal; no recognition of same-sex relationships but civil unions legislation was proposed during wartime.

CHN.note: Homosexuality is legal since 1997 but no anti-discrimination protections or relationship recognition; social acceptance is limited.

JPN.note: Homosexuality is legal; many municipalities offer partnership certificates. Courts have ruled the lack of marriage equality partly unconstitutional.

KOR.note: Homosexuality is legal; no anti-discrimination law. A 2024 Supreme Court ruling granted some benefits to same-sex partners.

IND.note: Homosexuality decriminalized in 2018; the Supreme Court declined to legalize same-sex marriage in 2023.

PAK.note: Homosexuality is criminalized with penalties up to life imprisonment; death penalty possible under Sharia law in some interpretations.

BGD.note: Homosexuality is criminalized under Section 377 with penalties up to life imprisonment.

IDN.note: Criminalized in Aceh province under Sharia law; not explicitly illegal nationally but facing increasing hostility.

MYS.note: Criminalized under both civil and Sharia law with penalties up to 20 years imprisonment and caning.

SGP.note: Section 377A repealed in 2022 decriminalizing homosexuality; constitution amended to prevent same-sex marriage.

THA.note: Marriage legal since January 2025; first Southeast Asian country. Anti-discrimination protections exist in employment.

VNM.note: Homosexuality is legal; same-sex weddings are no longer banned but have no legal recognition.

PHL.note: Homosexuality is legal; no anti-discrimination law or relationship recognition despite active advocacy.

AUS.note: Marriage legal since 2017; comprehensive anti-discrimination protections at federal and state levels.

NZL.note: Marriage legal since 2013; comprehensive Human Rights Act protections.

BRA.note: Marriage legal since 2013; Supreme Court criminalized homophobia in 2019.

ARG.note: Marriage legal since 2010; comprehensive gender identity law since 2012.

MEX.note: Marriage legal nationwide since 2022; anti-discrimination protections vary by state.

COL.note: Marriage legal since 2016; constitutional protections against discrimination.

CHL.note: Marriage legal since 2022; anti-discrimination law (Zamudio Law) covers sexual orientation since 2012.

ZAF.note: Marriage legal since 2006; the constitution explicitly prohibits discrimination on the basis of sexual orientation.

NGA.note: Same-Sex Marriage Prohibition Act imposes 14 years imprisonment; death by stoning possible in northern Sharia states.

EGY.note: No explicit law but prosecuted under debauchery and morality laws with imprisonment.

SAU.note: Homosexuality is punishable by death under Sharia law.

ARE.note: Criminalized with potential death penalty under federal law.

ISR.note: Homosexuality legal; marriages performed abroad recognized but not available domestically. Strong anti-discrimination protections.

IRN.note: Homosexuality is punishable by death under Sharia law; executions for same-sex conduct have been documented.

IRQ.note: A 2024 law criminalized same-sex relationships with up to 15 years imprisonment.

KEN.note: Criminalized with up to 14 years imprisonment; a 2019 High Court ruling upheld the colonial-era laws.

ETH.note: Criminalized with up to 15 years imprisonment.

MAR.note: Criminalized with 6 months to 3 years imprisonment.

TWN.note: Marriage legal since 2019; first in Asia. Anti-discrimination protections exist in employment and education.

BLR.note: Homosexuality is legal since 1994 but no anti-discrimination protections or relationship recognition.

topics.name: Immigration Openness
topics.description: How open a country's immigration and visa policies are — from very open to highly restrictive.

CAN.note: Points-based immigration system accepting over 400,000 permanent residents annually; visa-free for many nationalities.

USA.note: Complex visa system; significant backlogs for family and employment-based immigration. Enhanced border enforcement and asylum restrictions.

GBR.note: Points-based system post-Brexit; tightened in 2024 with higher salary thresholds. Plans to reduce net migration.

FRA.note: Selective immigration policy; the 2024 immigration law tightened requirements for family reunification and work permits.

DEU.note: Modernized immigration law in 2023 with a points-based system; Opportunity Card for job seekers. Accepted over 1 million refugees since 2015.

ITA.note: Restrictive immigration policies; the Cutro Decree (2023) and Albania processing center deal tightened asylum and irregular migration.

ESP.note: Relatively open; digital nomad visa introduced in 2023. Path to citizenship available after 10 years (2 for Latin Americans).

PRT.note: Historically open; golden visa program reformed in 2023. Digital nomad visa available.

NLD.note: Open for skilled workers; highly skilled migrant visa is streamlined. Asylum policies tightened in 2024.

BEL.note: Open for EU citizens; work permits required for non-EU nationals. Moderate asylum processing.

SWE.note: Tightened immigration significantly since 2016; shifted from one of Europe's most open to more restrictive.

NOR.note: Selective immigration; skilled worker visas available. Asylum policy has become more restrictive.

DNK.note: Among Europe's most restrictive immigration policies; strict family reunification rules and a zero-asylum seeker goal.

FIN.note: Actively recruiting skilled workers; streamlined work permits in 2023 but tightened asylum policies.

ISL.note: Open for EEA citizens; work permits required for others. Small but growing immigrant population.

CHE.note: Selective immigration; free movement with EU/EFTA. Non-EU immigration is restricted to skilled workers.

AUT.note: Red-White-Red Card system for skilled workers; immigration policies have tightened in recent years.

IRL.note: Open for EU citizens; Critical Skills Employment Permit attracts skilled workers.

POL.note: Became a major destination for Ukrainian refugees; work permits are accessible for non-EU nationals.

CZE.note: Moderate openness; work permits available but bureaucratic process. Significant Ukrainian refugee population.

HUN.note: Very restrictive on asylum; border fences and push-backs. Guest worker programs for selected nationalities.

ROU.note: Increasing openness to foreign workers to fill labor shortages; simplified work permit process.

BGR.note: Moderate openness; EU member with free movement for EU citizens. Limited non-EU immigration.

HRV.note: Growing openness to foreign workers since EU accession; simplified work permits for shortage occupations.

GRC.note: Golden visa program (lowered to EUR 250,000); tightened border controls but processes asylum claims.

TUR.note: Hosts over 3.5 million Syrian refugees (largest refugee population); relatively accessible work permits.

RUS.note: Complex visa and registration system; work permits available but bureaucratic. Selective immigration from CIS countries.

UKR.note: Relatively open visa-free access for many nationalities; simplified processes for skilled workers.

CHN.note: Very restrictive immigration; work permits are difficult to obtain and permanent residence is exceptionally rare.

JPN.note: Historically restrictive but opening up; Specified Skilled Worker visa expanded in 2024. Permanent residence remains difficult.

KOR.note: Selective immigration; Employment Permit System for low-skilled workers. Naturalization requirements are strict.

IND.note: Restrictive immigration; most visitors need a visa. No formal immigration program for settlement.

PAK.note: Limited immigration infrastructure; visa required for most nationalities with security screenings.

BGD.note: Limited immigration; visa required for most nationalities. Few pathways for permanent settlement.

IDN.note: Work permits require employer sponsorship; Second Home Visa introduced in 2022 for long-term stays.

MYS.note: Malaysia My Second Home program allows long-term residence; work permits available through employer sponsorship.

SGP.note: Open for skilled workers through Employment Pass; tightened with COMPASS framework in 2024. Foreign workers make up 40% of the workforce.

THA.note: Long-Term Resident visa launched in 2022; digital nomad visa available. Work permits require employer sponsorship.

VNM.note: Work permits required with employer sponsorship; limited pathways for permanent settlement.

PHL.note: Special Resident Retiree Visa and various work visas available; relatively accessible for retirees.

AUS.note: Points-based skilled migration; annual migration cap increased to 190,000. Working holiday visas widely available.

NZL.note: Skilled Migrant Category visa; Accredited Employer Work Visa streamlined in 2022.

BRA.note: Relatively open; digital nomad visa available. Humanitarian visas for Venezuelans, Haitians, and others.

ARG.note: Among the most open immigration policies; Mercosur citizens have easy residency. The constitution guarantees immigrant rights.

MEX.note: Temporary and permanent resident visas available; relatively accessible for retirees and remote workers.

COL.note: Digital nomad visa available; hosts over 2 million Venezuelan migrants with Temporary Protection Status.

CHL.note: New Migration Law (2021) modernized the system; work visas available with employer sponsorship.

ZAF.note: Complex and bureaucratic visa system; critical skills visa available but processing is slow.

NGA.note: Visa required for most nationalities; limited immigration pathways. ECOWAS citizens have free movement rights.

EGY.note: Visa on arrival for many nationalities for tourism; work permits and residency are more restrictive.

SAU.note: Large migrant workforce under the kafala (sponsorship) system; Premium Residency introduced but expensive. No path to citizenship.

ARE.note: Open for workers under employer sponsorship; Golden Visa for investors, professionals, and skilled workers. Foreign-born population exceeds 85%.

ISR.note: Law of Return grants citizenship to Jews; otherwise restrictive for non-Jewish immigration.

IRN.note: Very restrictive immigration; visa required for nearly all nationalities with extensive security checks.

IRQ.note: Visa required for most nationalities; limited immigration infrastructure.

KEN.note: E-visa system for most nationalities; East African Community citizens have free movement rights.

ETH.note: Visa required; e-visa available. Limited formal immigration pathways.

MAR.note: Visa-free for many nationalities; regularization programs for sub-Saharan African migrants in 2014 and 2017.

TWN.note: Gold Card for skilled professionals; Employment Gold Card expanded to attract talent. Work permits available through employers.

BLR.note: Visa required for most nationalities; limited immigration pathways. 30-day visa-free for some passport holders at Minsk airport.

topics.name: Encryption Regulation
topics.description: How countries regulate the use, sale, and export of encryption technology — from full protection to strict controls.

CAN.note: No restrictions on domestic encryption use; strong privacy protections under PIPEDA and the Charter of Rights.

USA.note: No domestic encryption restrictions but ongoing law enforcement push for backdoors; EARN IT Act debates continue. Export controls under EAR.

GBR.note: Investigatory Powers Act 2016 allows government to compel decryption; Online Safety Act 2023 pressures platforms to weaken E2E encryption.

FRA.note: Failure to comply with decryption orders is a criminal offense under Article 434-15-2 of the Penal Code; up to 3 years imprisonment.

DEU.note: Government actively promotes strong encryption; Federal Office for Information Security encourages end-to-end encryption adoption.

ITA.note: No domestic encryption restrictions; lawful intercept obligations exist for telecom providers but no key disclosure mandates.

ESP.note: No specific encryption restrictions; standard lawful intercept requirements for telecom operators under EU frameworks.

PRT.note: No encryption restrictions; standard EU data protection and lawful intercept frameworks apply.

NLD.note: Government has explicitly rejected encryption backdoors in a 2016 policy position; supports strong encryption for security.

BEL.note: No domestic encryption restrictions; lawful intercept obligations for telecom providers under EU frameworks.

SWE.note: No encryption restrictions for domestic use; signals intelligence agency FRA has broad surveillance powers but no key disclosure mandate.

NOR.note: No encryption restrictions; strong privacy tradition and no key disclosure mandates.

DNK.note: No domestic encryption restrictions; standard lawful intercept obligations for telecom providers.

FIN.note: No encryption restrictions; strong privacy protections and no mandatory key disclosure.

ISL.note: No encryption restrictions; strong digital rights protections.

CHE.note: No encryption restrictions; Switzerland hosts encrypted service providers like Proton and Threema.

AUT.note: No domestic encryption ban; 2024 surveillance legislation expanded lawful intercept capabilities but stopped short of mandating backdoors.

IRL.note: No encryption restrictions; hosts major tech companies and favors industry-aligned approach under EU Digital Single Market.

POL.note: No domestic encryption restrictions; lawful intercept obligations exist for telecom providers.

CZE.note: No encryption restrictions; standard EU lawful intercept frameworks apply.

HUN.note: Surveillance laws grant broad government access to communications; authorities can compel decryption assistance.

ROU.note: No specific encryption restrictions; standard lawful intercept frameworks under EU membership.

BGR.note: No specific encryption restrictions; lawful intercept obligations for telecom providers.

HRV.note: No encryption restrictions; standard EU lawful intercept frameworks apply.

GRC.note: No encryption restrictions; Predator spyware scandal in 2022 raised surveillance concerns but no encryption mandates.

TUR.note: Broad surveillance powers under anti-terror laws; VPN and encryption services periodically blocked. Government can compel decryption.

RUS.note: Yarovaya Law requires telecom providers to store and decrypt communications on demand; FSB has mandatory key escrow powers.

UKR.note: No specific encryption restrictions; wartime security measures have increased surveillance but no encryption bans.

CHN.note: Encryption Law (2020) requires commercial encryption products to be approved; companies must provide technical assistance for decryption to authorities.

JPN.note: No domestic encryption restrictions; lawful intercept capabilities exist but no mandatory backdoor or key disclosure laws.

KOR.note: No encryption restrictions for domestic use; lawful intercept requirements for telecom providers.

IND.note: IT Act Section 69 allows government to order decryption; intermediary guidelines require traceability of messages.

PAK.note: Prevention of Electronic Crimes Act grants broad surveillance powers; authorities can compel decryption of communications.

BGD.note: Digital Security Act grants government broad powers to intercept communications and compel decryption.

IDN.note: Government Regulation 71/2019 requires electronic system operators to provide access to encrypted data upon request.

MYS.note: No specific encryption restrictions; lawful intercept obligations exist under communications legislation.

SGP.note: No encryption restrictions for domestic use; Cybersecurity Act 2018 allows government to compel technical assistance but has not mandated backdoors.

THA.note: Computer Crime Act and Cybersecurity Act grant broad government powers to access encrypted communications.

VNM.note: Cybersecurity Law (2018) requires data localization and companies must provide user data to authorities, effectively restricting strong encryption.

PHL.note: No specific encryption restrictions; lawful intercept provisions exist under anti-wiretapping and cybercrime laws.

AUS.note: Assistance and Access Act 2018 allows government to compel companies to provide technical assistance to bypass encryption.

NZL.note: GCSB and TICS Acts allow government to compel decryption assistance from network operators and service providers.

BRA.note: Courts have repeatedly ordered WhatsApp to decrypt messages; Marco Civil da Internet allows judicial decryption orders.

ARG.note: No specific encryption restrictions; lawful intercept provisions exist but no key disclosure mandates.

MEX.note: No encryption restrictions; lawful intercept requirements exist for telecom providers under federal telecommunications law.

COL.note: No specific encryption restrictions; standard lawful intercept obligations for telecom providers.

CHL.note: No encryption restrictions; standard lawful intercept frameworks apply.

ZAF.note: RICA Act requires registration and lawful intercept capability; authorities can compel decryption under certain circumstances.

NGA.note: Cybercrimes Act 2015 grants broad powers to compel service providers to assist in decryption.

EGY.note: Anti-Cyber and Information Technology Crimes Law restricts encryption; VPN use without approval can be penalized.

SAU.note: Broad surveillance powers; encryption tools require government approval and VoIP encryption has been restricted.

ARE.note: Cybercrime law restricts use of VPNs and encryption for illegal purposes; VoIP services are heavily restricted.

ISR.note: No domestic encryption restrictions; strong tech sector uses and exports encryption. Lawful intercept capabilities exist.

IRN.note: VPNs and encrypted messaging apps frequently blocked; government requires ISPs to filter and monitor traffic.

IRQ.note: Internet restrictions and periodic social media bans; authorities assert power to intercept communications.

KEN.note: No specific encryption restrictions; Computer Misuse and Cybercrimes Act has broad provisions but no key disclosure mandates.

ETH.note: Telecom Fraud Offences Proclamation restricts VoIP and certain encrypted services; Ethio Telecom monopoly enables monitoring.

MAR.note: VoIP services periodically blocked; government has surveillance capabilities and can compel telecom cooperation.

TWN.note: No encryption restrictions; strong digital rights protections and thriving tech sector.

BLR.note: Broad government surveillance powers; encryption and VPN use restricted, especially after 2020 protests crackdown.

topics.name: Surrogacy
topics.description: How countries regulate gestational and traditional surrogacy — from legal commercial arrangements to full prohibition.

CAN.note: Altruistic surrogacy legal under the Assisted Human Reproduction Act; commercial surrogacy (payment beyond expenses) is a criminal offense.

USA.note: Varies by state; commercial surrogacy legal and regulated in many states including California, Nevada, and others. Some states restrict or ban it.

GBR.note: Altruistic surrogacy legal; commercial surrogacy banned. Surrogates are legal mothers at birth; intended parents must obtain a parental order.

FRA.note: All forms of surrogacy are prohibited under bioethics laws; surrogacy contracts are void and unenforceable.

DEU.note: Surrogacy is prohibited under the Embryo Protection Act 1990; both commercial and altruistic surrogacy are banned.

ITA.note: All surrogacy banned; a 2024 law extended the ban to prosecute Italian citizens who pursue surrogacy abroad.

ESP.note: Surrogacy contracts are null and void under the Assisted Reproduction Act; surrogacy is effectively prohibited.

PRT.note: Altruistic gestational surrogacy was legalized in 2016 but implementation stalled after Constitutional Court rulings; limited framework in practice.

NLD.note: No specific surrogacy legislation; commercial surrogacy is not explicitly permitted, altruistic arrangements occur in practice.

BEL.note: No specific surrogacy law; some hospitals facilitate altruistic gestational surrogacy under ethical committee oversight.

SWE.note: Surrogacy is not explicitly regulated but effectively prohibited; 2016 government inquiry recommended against legalizing it.

NOR.note: Surrogacy is prohibited under the Biotechnology Act; egg donation is also banned, making gestational surrogacy impossible.

DNK.note: Altruistic surrogacy is tolerated in limited circumstances; commercial surrogacy is prohibited. No comprehensive legislation.

FIN.note: No specific surrogacy law but effectively banned; assisted reproduction law does not accommodate surrogacy arrangements.

ISL.note: Surrogacy is not regulated and effectively unavailable; no legal framework exists for surrogacy arrangements.

CHE.note: Surrogacy is prohibited under the Federal Constitution (Article 119) and the Reproductive Medicine Act.

AUT.note: Surrogacy is prohibited under the Reproductive Medicine Act; surrogacy agreements are null and void.

IRL.note: No legislation specifically addressing surrogacy; altruistic arrangements occur but lack legal framework. Health (Assisted Human Reproduction) Act 2024 introduced some regulation.

POL.note: No specific surrogacy legislation; the practice occurs in a legal gray area without explicit prohibition or regulation.

CZE.note: No specific surrogacy law; altruistic surrogacy occurs in practice without formal regulation.

HUN.note: Surrogacy is prohibited under the Health Act; surrogacy contracts are null and void.

ROU.note: Surrogacy is not explicitly regulated but considered incompatible with existing civil law; effectively prohibited.

BGR.note: No specific surrogacy legislation but effectively prohibited under existing health and family law.

HRV.note: Surrogacy is prohibited under the Medically Assisted Reproduction Act.

GRC.note: Altruistic gestational surrogacy is legal under strict conditions since 2002; surrogate must be under 50 and intended mother must have medical need.

TUR.note: All forms of surrogacy are prohibited under the Regulation on Assisted Reproductive Treatment Practices.

RUS.note: Commercial surrogacy was legal and Russia was a major surrogacy destination; restrictions tightened in 2022 banning surrogacy for foreigners.

UKR.note: Commercial surrogacy legal for married heterosexual couples with medical indication; one of the world's most popular surrogacy destinations.

CHN.note: Surrogacy is prohibited under the Ministry of Health guidelines; underground commercial surrogacy persists despite bans.

JPN.note: No explicit law but the Japan Society of Obstetrics and Gynecology guidelines prohibit surrogacy; effectively banned.

KOR.note: No specific surrogacy legislation; the practice exists in a legal gray area without explicit prohibition.

IND.note: Surrogacy (Regulation) Act 2021 permits only altruistic surrogacy for Indian married couples; commercial surrogacy banned.

PAK.note: No specific legislation but surrogacy is considered impermissible under Islamic jurisprudence.

BGD.note: No specific legislation but surrogacy is not practiced due to religious and social norms.

IDN.note: Surrogacy is prohibited under health law and Islamic legal principles that dominate reproductive policy.

MYS.note: No specific legislation but surrogacy is effectively prohibited for Muslims under Islamic law and unavailable in practice.

SGP.note: No specific surrogacy law but the Ministry of Health does not permit surrogacy arrangements in licensed clinics.

THA.note: Protection of Children Born Through Assisted Reproductive Technologies Act 2015 permits altruistic surrogacy for Thai married couples only.

VNM.note: Altruistic surrogacy is permitted for relatives under the 2014 Marriage and Family Law; commercial surrogacy is prohibited.

PHL.note: No specific legislation but surrogacy is not practiced or available; reproductive health law does not address surrogacy.

AUS.note: Altruistic surrogacy legal in most states; commercial surrogacy is a criminal offense. Laws vary by state and territory.

NZL.note: Altruistic surrogacy permitted; commercial surrogacy prohibited under the Human Assisted Reproductive Technology Act 2004.

BRA.note: Altruistic surrogacy permitted under Federal Medical Council resolution; surrogate must be a family member unless authorized by the council.

ARG.note: No specific surrogacy legislation; courts have recognized surrogacy agreements on a case-by-case basis.

MEX.note: Varies by state; Tabasco and Sinaloa have frameworks permitting surrogacy. Most states have no specific legislation.

COL.note: No specific surrogacy legislation; courts have addressed surrogacy disputes but no comprehensive regulation exists.

CHL.note: No specific legislation but surrogacy is not available; assisted reproduction law does not contemplate surrogacy.

ZAF.note: Altruistic surrogacy regulated under Children's Act 2005; requires court pre-approval. Commercial surrogacy prohibited.

NGA.note: No specific surrogacy legislation; the practice exists in an unregulated space.

EGY.note: Surrogacy is prohibited under Islamic jurisprudence as interpreted by Al-Azhar and Egyptian law.

SAU.note: Surrogacy is prohibited under Islamic law; all major Saudi fatwas declare surrogacy impermissible.

ARE.note: Surrogacy is prohibited under Federal Law; not permitted under the UAE's interpretation of Islamic jurisprudence.

ISR.note: Altruistic surrogacy legal since 1996 under the Surrogacy Agreements Act; expanded in 2024 to include same-sex couples. Commercial surrogacy abroad is common.

IRN.note: Altruistic gestational surrogacy is permitted under Shia jurisprudence and regulated by the Embryo Donation Act.

IRQ.note: Surrogacy is considered impermissible under Islamic law and is not practiced.

KEN.note: No specific surrogacy legislation; the practice exists in an unregulated legal environment.

ETH.note: No specific legislation but surrogacy is not practiced due to cultural and religious norms.

MAR.note: Surrogacy is prohibited under Islamic law principles that inform Moroccan family law.

TWN.note: No specific surrogacy legislation; draft bills have been proposed but not enacted. Some altruistic arrangements occur.

BLR.note: Commercial surrogacy is legal and regulated under the 2012 Law on Assisted Reproductive Technologies.

topics.name: Right to Repair
topics.description: Whether consumers and independent shops have the legal right to repair products — from strong protections to manufacturer lock-in.

CAN.note: Quebec passed right to repair legislation in 2023; federal efforts ongoing. Other provinces considering similar laws.

USA.note: FTC right to repair enforcement policy since 2021; multiple states have passed laws including California, Minnesota, New York, and Oregon.

GBR.note: Ecodesign regulations since 2021 require manufacturers to make spare parts available for certain appliances for up to 10 years.

FRA.note: French repairability index mandatory since 2021; anti-waste law (AGEC) requires spare parts availability and repairability scoring for electronics.

DEU.note: EU right to repair directive transposed in 2024; strong consumer protection tradition supports repair access.

ITA.note: EU right to repair directive applies; Italy implementing ecodesign and repairability requirements.

ESP.note: EU right to repair directive applies; Spain has been active in supporting EU-level right to repair initiatives.

PRT.note: EU right to repair directive applies; limited domestic legislation beyond EU requirements.

NLD.note: EU right to repair directive applies; Netherlands has been a vocal proponent of repair rights at EU level.

BEL.note: EU right to repair directive applies; Belgium has supported reduced VAT on repair services.

SWE.note: Reduced VAT on repairs since 2017; tax deductions for repair labor. EU right to repair directive also applies.

NOR.note: EEA agreement extends many EU ecodesign requirements; emerging repair culture but limited specific legislation.

DNK.note: EU right to repair directive applies; Denmark supports EU-level repairability requirements.

FIN.note: EU right to repair directive applies; Finland has focused on circular economy policies supporting repair.

ISL.note: Limited specific right to repair legislation; small market limits policy focus on this issue.

CHE.note: No specific right to repair legislation; not bound by EU directives. General consumer protection laws apply.

AUT.note: EU right to repair directive applies; Austria introduced a repair bonus subsidy program in 2022.

IRL.note: EU right to repair directive applies; Ireland has implemented EU ecodesign regulations.

POL.note: EU right to repair directive applies; implementation of EU-wide repairability requirements.

CZE.note: EU right to repair directive applies; Czech Republic implementing EU ecodesign requirements.

HUN.note: EU right to repair directive applies; limited domestic initiatives beyond EU requirements.

ROU.note: EU right to repair directive applies; implementation of EU-wide requirements.

BGR.note: EU right to repair directive applies; limited domestic repair infrastructure.

HRV.note: EU right to repair directive applies; implementation of EU ecodesign requirements.

GRC.note: EU right to repair directive applies; implementation of EU-wide requirements.

TUR.note: No specific right to repair legislation; consumer protection law provides general warranty provisions.

RUS.note: No specific right to repair legislation; parallel import legalization in 2022 improved parts availability after sanctions.

UKR.note: No specific right to repair legislation; wartime priorities have focused attention on other policy areas.

CHN.note: No specific right to repair legislation; manufacturers generally control repair ecosystems. Robust third-party repair market exists informally.

JPN.note: No specific right to repair legislation; consumer electronics manufacturers maintain proprietary repair networks.

KOR.note: No specific right to repair legislation; some voluntary industry commitments to provide spare parts.

IND.note: Bureau of Indian Standards framework for repairability introduced in 2024; mandatory repairability index for electronics under development.

PAK.note: No specific right to repair legislation; vibrant informal repair economy exists.

BGD.note: No specific right to repair legislation; strong informal repair sector driven by economic necessity.

IDN.note: No specific right to repair legislation; significant informal repair economy.

MYS.note: No specific right to repair legislation; some consumer protection provisions apply.

SGP.note: No specific right to repair legislation; focus on e-waste management rather than repairability.

THA.note: No specific right to repair legislation; informal repair sector is widespread.

VNM.note: No specific right to repair legislation; large informal repair economy.

PHL.note: No specific right to repair legislation; informal repair sector serves consumer needs.

AUS.note: Productivity Commission report in 2024 recommended right to repair reforms; Australian Consumer Law already provides some protections.

NZL.note: No specific right to repair legislation; Consumer Guarantees Act provides some general protections.

BRA.note: No specific right to repair legislation; Consumer Defense Code provides general warranty protections.

ARG.note: No specific right to repair legislation; consumer protection law provides general warranty provisions.

MEX.note: No specific right to repair legislation; strong informal repair culture exists.

COL.note: No specific right to repair legislation; general consumer protection provisions apply.

CHL.note: No specific right to repair legislation; general consumer protection law applies.

ZAF.note: No specific right to repair legislation; Consumer Protection Act provides general warranty rights.

NGA.note: No specific right to repair legislation; vibrant informal repair economy.

EGY.note: No specific right to repair legislation; large informal repair sector.

SAU.note: No specific right to repair legislation; warranty obligations under commercial law.

ARE.note: No specific right to repair legislation; consumer protection law provides basic warranty rights.

ISR.note: No specific right to repair legislation; general consumer protection law applies.

IRN.note: No specific right to repair legislation; sanctions have created a necessity-driven repair culture.

IRQ.note: No specific right to repair legislation; informal repair economy exists.

KEN.note: No specific right to repair legislation; strong informal repair sector.

ETH.note: No specific right to repair legislation; repair is common practice due to economic conditions.

MAR.note: No specific right to repair legislation; general consumer protection provisions.

TWN.note: No specific right to repair legislation; consumer protection law provides general warranty provisions.

BLR.note: No specific right to repair legislation; general consumer protection provisions apply.

topics.name: Drug Decriminalization
topics.description: How countries handle drug possession for personal use — from full decriminalization to the death penalty.

CAN.note: Cannabis legal; British Columbia decriminalized possession of small amounts of all drugs in 2023 as a pilot, though paused public use exemption in 2024.

USA.note: Federal drug scheduling remains strict; Oregon recriminalized in 2024 after its 2020 decriminalization. Cannabis legal in many states.

GBR.note: Misuse of Drugs Act 1971 criminalizes all listed substances; drug consumption rooms under discussion but not implemented nationally.

FRA.note: Drug use is a criminal offense; 2020 reform introduced fixed fines for cannabis. Hard drug possession carries criminal penalties.

DEU.note: Cannabis legalized in 2024; prosecutors may decline to prosecute small-quantity personal possession of other drugs under BtMG Section 31a.

ITA.note: Personal possession of all drugs is an administrative offense since 1990s reforms; criminal penalties apply only to dealing and trafficking.

ESP.note: Personal drug use in private is not criminalized; public use is an administrative offense. Drug consumption rooms operate in several cities.

PRT.note: All drugs decriminalized for personal use since 2001; possession of up to 10-day supply is handled by dissuasion commissions, not courts.

NLD.note: Cannabis tolerated in coffee shops; other drugs technically illegal but prosecution for small personal possession is rare.

BEL.note: Cannabis possession of up to 3g is lowest police priority; other drug use is technically criminal but focus is on trafficking.

SWE.note: Zero-tolerance approach; personal use of all drugs is criminalized. Sweden has among the strictest drug policies in Europe.

NOR.note: Drug reform commission proposed decriminalization in 2021; parliament rejected full decriminalization but expanded health-based approaches.

DNK.note: Drug use is not a criminal offense but possession is; supervised consumption facilities operate in Copenhagen.

FIN.note: All drug possession is criminalized; petty drug offenses can result in fines. Harm reduction services available.

ISL.note: Drug possession is criminalized; parliament has discussed decriminalization but no legislation passed.

CHE.note: Cannabis under 10g decriminalized with fine; heroin-assisted treatment programs since 1994. Pragmatic harm reduction approach.

AUT.note: Personal use possession can be diverted to treatment instead of prosecution under the SMG; health-oriented approach.

IRL.note: Drug possession is criminal; Citizens' Assembly on Drug Use (2023) recommended decriminalization but legislation pending.

POL.note: Drug possession is criminal; prosecutors may waive charges for insignificant personal amounts under 2011 amendment.

CZE.note: Possession of small amounts for personal use is a misdemeanor since 2010; cannabis legalized in 2026. Drug consumption rooms operate.

HUN.note: Strict criminal penalties for drug possession; even small amounts can result in imprisonment up to 2 years.

ROU.note: Drug possession is criminalized with potential imprisonment; no decriminalization measures.

BGR.note: Drug possession is criminalized; penalties range from 1 to 6 years imprisonment.

HRV.note: Small amounts for personal use are a misdemeanor punishable by fine since 2013 amendments.

GRC.note: Drug use is criminalized; 2013 reform introduced lighter penalties for personal use but criminal records still apply.

TUR.note: Drug use and possession carry criminal penalties; courts may defer prosecution if offender enters treatment.

RUS.note: Drug possession is criminal; small amounts are administrative offenses but thresholds are very low, effectively criminalizing most possession.

UKR.note: Drug possession is criminal; small amounts treated as administrative offense. Medical cannabis legalized in 2024.

CHN.note: Drug possession is criminal with severe penalties; trafficking can carry the death penalty. Forced rehabilitation for users.

JPN.note: Strict criminal penalties for drug possession; even cannabis possession can result in up to 5 years imprisonment.

KOR.note: Strict criminal approach; drug possession punishable by up to 5 years imprisonment. Extraterritorial jurisdiction for citizens.

IND.note: NDPS Act criminalizes possession; small quantity offenses carry up to 1 year imprisonment. Trafficking can carry death penalty.

PAK.note: Control of Narcotic Substances Act criminalizes all drug possession; death penalty possible for trafficking.

BGD.note: Narcotics Control Act imposes severe penalties; trafficking can carry the death penalty.

IDN.note: Severe penalties including death for trafficking; personal use can lead to lengthy imprisonment or forced rehabilitation.

MYS.note: Dangerous Drugs Act imposes severe penalties; presumption of trafficking above threshold amounts. Mandatory death penalty ended in 2023.

SGP.note: Misuse of Drugs Act imposes severe penalties; death penalty mandatory for trafficking above prescribed amounts.

THA.note: Cannabis removed from narcotics list in 2022; methamphetamine penalties reduced. Reform towards health-based approach ongoing.

VNM.note: Severe penalties for drug offenses; trafficking can carry the death penalty. Forced rehabilitation for users.

PHL.note: Comprehensive Dangerous Drugs Act imposes severe penalties; anti-drug campaign resulted in thousands of extrajudicial killings since 2016.

AUS.note: ACT decriminalized small amounts of most drugs in 2023; other states use diversion programs. Cannabis decriminalized in several states.

NZL.note: Drug possession is criminal; 2019 amendment directs police to use discretion and consider health-based responses.

BRA.note: Supreme Court decriminalized personal drug possession in 2024 for quantities up to prescribed amounts; users directed to treatment.

ARG.note: Supreme Court ruled personal drug possession for private use unconstitutional to punish in 2009; practical enforcement varies.

MEX.note: Personal possession of small amounts decriminalized since 2009 via threshold table; cannabis use ruled constitutional in 2021.

COL.note: Personal dose doctrine decriminalizes possession of small amounts; constitutional protection for personal use since 1994.

CHL.note: Private drug use not criminalized; public use and possession for trafficking are criminal. Law 20000 distinguishes personal use.

ZAF.note: Cannabis for personal use in private is legal since 2018 Constitutional Court ruling; other drugs remain criminalized.

NGA.note: NDLEA Act criminalizes drug possession; penalties include lengthy imprisonment. No decriminalization measures.

EGY.note: Drug possession is criminalized under narcotics law; penalties include imprisonment and hard labor for trafficking.

SAU.note: Severe penalties under Sharia-influenced law; drug trafficking can result in public execution by beheading.

ARE.note: Severe penalties for drug offenses; minimum 4 years imprisonment for possession. Death penalty possible for trafficking.

ISR.note: Cannabis decriminalized for personal use since 2017; police discretion policy for other drug users emphasizes treatment.

IRN.note: Severe penalties including death for drug trafficking; Iran carries out among the most drug-related executions globally.

IRQ.note: Severe criminal penalties for drug offenses; death penalty applicable for trafficking.

KEN.note: Narcotic Drugs and Psychotropic Substances Act criminalizes possession; penalties include up to 20 years imprisonment.

ETH.note: Drug possession is criminalized; khat use is culturally accepted and legal. Other drugs carry criminal penalties.

MAR.note: Drug possession is criminal; cannabis cultivation was partially legalized for medical/industrial use in 2021.

TWN.note: Narcotics Hazard Prevention Act criminalizes possession; different tiers of substances carry different penalties.

BLR.note: Severe penalties for drug offenses; trafficking carries up to 25 years or death penalty.

topics.name: Renewable Energy Targets
topics.description: Government commitments to renewable energy and decarbonization — from ambitious net-zero timelines to fossil fuel dependence.

CAN.note: Legally binding net-zero by 2050 under the Canadian Net-Zero Emissions Accountability Act 2021; carbon pricing in place.

USA.note: Rejoined Paris Agreement; Inflation Reduction Act 2022 provides major clean energy subsidies. Net zero 2050 target but not legislated.

GBR.note: Legally binding net-zero by 2050 under the Climate Change Act 2008 (amended 2019); coal phase-out completed 2024.

FRA.note: Legally binding net-zero by 2050 under the Climate and Resilience Law 2021; heavy reliance on nuclear energy.

DEU.note: Legally binding climate neutrality by 2045 under the Federal Climate Change Act; ambitious Energiewende transition.

ITA.note: EU binding net-zero by 2050 target; National Energy and Climate Plan targets 30% renewables by 2030.

ESP.note: Climate Change Law 2021 sets net-zero by 2050; targeting 74% renewable electricity by 2030.
