PRT.note: Carbon neutrality by 2050 target in law; Portugal regularly exceeds 60% renewable electricity generation.

NLD.note: Climate Act 2019 sets 95% emissions reduction by 2050; major offshore wind expansion planned.

BEL.note: EU binding net-zero by 2050; National Energy and Climate Plan targets vary by region.

SWE.note: Net-zero by 2045 legislated under the Climate Policy Framework 2017; already over 65% renewable energy.

NOR.note: Climate Change Act targets 90-95% reduction by 2050; major oil/gas producer but electricity nearly 100% hydropower.

DNK.note: Climate Act 2020 targets 70% reduction by 2030 and net-zero by 2050; aim for climate neutrality well before 2050.

FIN.note: Climate Change Act targets carbon neutrality by 2035, among the most ambitious globally.

ISL.note: Carbon neutrality by 2040 target; nearly 100% renewable electricity from geothermal and hydropower.

CHE.note: Net-zero by 2050 target adopted via referendum in 2023 under the Climate and Innovation Act.

AUT.note: 100% renewable electricity by 2030 target under the Renewable Energy Expansion Act; climate neutrality by 2040.

IRL.note: Climate Action and Low Carbon Development Act 2021 targets net-zero by 2050; 80% renewable electricity by 2030.

POL.note: EU binding 2050 target applies but Poland heavily coal-dependent; energy policy prioritizes energy security over rapid transition.

CZE.note: EU binding 2050 target; heavily reliant on coal and nuclear. Climate targets seen as challenging given energy mix.

HUN.note: EU binding 2050 target; climate neutrality by 2050 in law but limited renewable ambition. Relies on Paks nuclear expansion.

ROU.note: EU binding 2050 target; significant coal and gas reliance. Offshore wind development in Black Sea emerging.

BGR.note: EU binding 2050 target; heavily coal-dependent with slow transition progress.

HRV.note: EU binding net-zero by 2050; already generates significant hydropower. National plan targets 37% renewables by 2030.

GRC.note: EU binding 2050 target; rapid coal phase-out and major solar/wind expansion. Targeting 80% renewable electricity by 2030.

TUR.note: Net-zero by 2053 target declared but not legislated; one of the last countries to ratify Paris Agreement in 2021.

RUS.note: Carbon neutrality by 2060 target but no binding legislation; economy heavily dependent on oil and gas exports.

UKR.note: Pre-war 2050 carbon neutrality target; energy infrastructure severely damaged. Reconstruction plans include renewable expansion.

CHN.note: Carbon neutrality by 2060 pledge; world's largest renewable energy installer but also largest coal consumer and emitter.

JPN.note: Net-zero by 2050 legislated; Green Transformation (GX) Act 2023 provides transition framework. Restarting nuclear reactors.

KOR.note: Carbon neutrality by 2050 legislated under Framework Act on Carbon Neutrality 2021; expanding renewable and nuclear capacity.

IND.note: Net-zero by 2070 pledge; 500 GW non-fossil fuel capacity by 2030 target. Still heavily coal-dependent.

PAK.note: Net-zero by 2050 pledge but not legislated; 60% renewable energy by 2030 target set in updated NDC.

BGD.note: Limited renewable targets; heavily dependent on natural gas and coal. Vulnerable to climate change impacts.

IDN.note: Net-zero by 2060 target; Just Energy Transition Partnership (JETP) aims to peak emissions by 2030.

MYS.note: Net-zero by 2050 aspiration; National Energy Transition Roadway targets 70% renewable capacity by 2050.

SGP.note: Net-zero by 2050 target; Singapore Green Plan 2030 sets interim targets. Limited domestic renewable potential.

THA.note: Carbon neutrality by 2050 and net-zero by 2065 targets; Power Development Plan includes renewable expansion.

VNM.note: Net-zero by 2050 pledge at COP26; Power Development Plan VIII targets 30-39% renewables by 2030.

PHL.note: Renewable Energy Act targets but limited binding commitments; 35% renewable share in power generation by 2030.

AUS.note: Net-zero by 2050 legislated under Climate Change Act 2022; 82% renewables in electricity by 2030 target.

NZL.note: Net-zero by 2050 legislated under the Zero Carbon Act 2019; already ~85% renewable electricity from hydro and geothermal.

BRA.note: Net-zero by 2050 pledge; electricity already ~85% renewable (hydro, wind, solar). Major reforestation commitments.

ARG.note: Net-zero by 2050 pledge; Renewable Energy Law targets 20% renewables by 2025. Large fossil fuel reserves (Vaca Muerta).

MEX.note: Paris Agreement commitments but energy policy has favored state-owned fossil fuel companies under recent administrations.

COL.note: Net-zero by 2050 target in Energy Transition Law; ambitious plan to halt new oil exploration.

CHL.note: Carbon neutrality by 2050 in law; coal phase-out by 2040 target. Leading Latin American renewable energy installer.

ZAF.note: Net-zero by 2050 aspiration in updated NDC; heavily coal-dependent. JETP funding for transition.

NGA.note: Net-zero by 2060 pledge; Energy Transition Plan announced but heavily reliant on oil revenues.

EGY.note: 42% renewable electricity by 2035 target; hosted COP27. Large-scale solar and wind projects underway.

SAU.note: Net-zero by 2060 target but world's largest oil exporter; NEOM green hydrogen projects are notable exceptions.

ARE.note: Net-zero by 2050 target; major solar investments including world's largest solar plant. Still major oil producer.

ISR.note: 30% renewable electricity by 2030 target; natural gas dominant in electricity mix. Solar expansion accelerating.

IRN.note: No significant renewable targets; economy dependent on oil and gas. Limited renewable development despite solar potential.

IRQ.note: No binding renewable targets; oil-dependent economy with severe electricity shortages. Some solar projects emerging.

KEN.note: Already generates ~90% renewable electricity (geothermal, hydro, wind); targeting 100% renewable electricity by 2030.

ETH.note: Grand Ethiopian Renaissance Dam and major wind/geothermal projects; Climate Resilient Green Economy Strategy targets carbon neutrality.

MAR.note: 52% renewable capacity by 2030 target; Noor-Ouarzazate solar complex is Africa's largest. Strong renewable commitment.

TWN.note: Net-zero by 2050 legislated under Climate Change Response Act 2023; 20% renewable electricity target by 2025.

BLR.note: No significant renewable targets; dependent on Russian gas and Ostrovets nuclear plant. Limited renewable development.

topics.name: Facial Recognition Regulation
topics.description: How countries regulate government and commercial use of facial recognition technology — from outright bans to pervasive deployment.

CAN.note: Privacy Commissioner found Clearview AI violated PIPEDA; federal and provincial privacy laws regulate use. No comprehensive facial recognition law.

USA.note: No federal regulation; patchwork of state/city laws. Several cities banned government use (San Francisco, Boston). Illinois BIPA regulates commercial use.

GBR.note: Police use regulated by court rulings and data protection law; Court of Appeal ruled South Wales Police use unlawful in 2020. Oversight improving.

FRA.note: CNIL strictly regulates facial recognition; EU AI Act prohibits real-time biometric surveillance in public spaces with narrow exceptions.

DEU.note: Strong data protection under GDPR and Federal Data Protection Act; EU AI Act bans real-time public facial recognition. Very restrictive approach.

ITA.note: Garante privacy authority banned Clearview AI in 2022; EU AI Act applies. Moratorium on public facial recognition systems.

ESP.note: AEPD data protection authority enforces GDPR restrictions; EU AI Act ban on real-time public biometric surveillance applies.

PRT.note: GDPR and EU AI Act apply; limited deployment of facial recognition by law enforcement.

NLD.note: Dutch DPA fined Clearview AI; GDPR restrictions strictly enforced. EU AI Act real-time biometric surveillance ban applies.

BEL.note: EU AI Act applies with strict implementation; Belgian DPA has been aggressive on biometric data enforcement. Effectively banned in public spaces.

SWE.note: Swedish DPA fined police for using Clearview AI in 2021; GDPR and EU AI Act provide strict framework.

NOR.note: Data Protection Authority enforces GDPR restrictions; no specific facial recognition law but strong privacy oversight.

DNK.note: GDPR applies; limited law enforcement use with oversight. EU AI Act restrictions on biometric surveillance apply.

FIN.note: GDPR applies; limited police use under strict conditions. National AI strategy emphasizes ethical AI development.

ISL.note: EEA GDPR framework applies; very limited deployment of facial recognition technology.

CHE.note: Federal Data Protection Act revised in 2023 provides strong biometric data protections; no specific facial recognition ban.

AUT.note: GDPR and EU AI Act apply; Austrian DPA has enforced biometric data restrictions.

IRL.note: GDPR enforced by DPC; EU AI Act applies. Garda use of facial recognition debated but limited.

POL.note: GDPR applies but enforcement weaker; some police facial recognition deployments without comprehensive legal framework.

CZE.note: GDPR and EU AI Act apply; limited deployment with emerging regulatory framework.

HUN.note: Limited regulation despite EU membership; government surveillance capabilities have expanded under Orbán administration.

ROU.note: GDPR applies but limited enforcement on facial recognition specifically; some police use without comprehensive framework.

BGR.note: GDPR applies but limited enforcement; growing use of surveillance technology with limited oversight.

HRV.note: GDPR and EU AI Act apply; limited deployment of facial recognition systems.

GRC.note: GDPR applies; Hellenic DPA provides oversight. EU AI Act restrictions apply.

TUR.note: Personal Data Protection Law provides some regulation; growing deployment for security purposes with limited independent oversight.

RUS.note: Moscow operates one of the world's largest facial recognition systems with 200,000+ cameras; used for surveillance of protesters. Minimal privacy safeguards.

UKR.note: Clearview AI used extensively during wartime for identifying casualties and combatants; limited peacetime regulatory framework.

CHN.note: World's largest facial recognition deployment; hundreds of millions of cameras in social control infrastructure. 2023 regulation limits commercial but not government use.

JPN.note: Personal Information Protection Act provides some regulation; NPA uses facial recognition for law enforcement with limited public oversight.

KOR.note: Personal Information Protection Act regulates biometric data; 2024 amendments strengthen consent requirements for facial recognition.

IND.note: Rapidly expanding deployment for law enforcement and Aadhaar identification; limited regulation. Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023 provides basic framework.

PAK.note: Growing use of facial recognition for security; Safe City projects in Islamabad and Lahore. Limited regulatory framework.

BGD.note: Limited deployment; some use for national ID verification. No specific regulatory framework.

IDN.note: Growing deployment for border control and smart city initiatives; limited data protection regulation.

MYS.note: Expanding use for border control and security; Personal Data Protection Act provides limited regulation.

SGP.note: Widespread deployment in Changi Airport and public housing; Personal Data Protection Act applies. Government promotes Smart Nation initiative.

THA.note: Growing deployment for security; limited regulation under Personal Data Protection Act 2019.

VNM.note: Government expanding facial recognition for population surveillance; Decree 13/2023 mandates face authentication for online accounts.

PHL.note: Limited deployment; Data Privacy Act provides basic framework but no specific facial recognition regulation.

AUS.note: Privacy Act provides some regulation; proposed Identity Verification Services Bill debated. Clearview AI found to breach privacy law in 2021.

NZL.note: Privacy Act 2020 provides strong framework; limited police use. Privacy Commissioner provides oversight.

BRA.note: LGPD provides data protection framework; São Paulo metro and several cities use facial recognition. Regulation evolving.

ARG.note: Buenos Aires deployed facial recognition for fugitive identification; court ordered suspension in 2022 over civil liberties concerns.

MEX.note: Growing deployment in Mexico City and other areas; limited regulatory framework for biometric surveillance.

COL.note: Police use expanding; limited specific regulation. Data protection law provides general framework.

CHL.note: Growing deployment; data protection framework provides basic regulation. No specific facial recognition legislation.

ZAF.note: POPIA provides data protection framework; growing use of facial recognition in private security and retail.

NGA.note: National Identity Management Commission uses biometrics; limited regulation of facial recognition.

EGY.note: Expanding surveillance infrastructure; facial recognition deployed at airports and public spaces with no independent oversight.

SAU.note: Deployed for Hajj crowd management and smart city projects (NEOM); limited public transparency on surveillance use.

ARE.note: Extensive deployment in Dubai for smart city and security; limited public transparency. Used in airports and public spaces.

ISR.note: Deployed in West Bank for surveillance; domestic use regulated by Privacy Protection Authority. Military use extensive.

IRN.note: Facial recognition deployed to enforce hijab laws since 2022; surveillance cameras in public transport and streets for morality enforcement.

IRQ.note: Limited deployment for security checkpoints; no specific regulatory framework.

KEN.note: Huduma Namba digital ID system includes biometrics; limited regulation of facial recognition technology.

ETH.note: Limited deployment; digital ID project includes biometrics. No specific facial recognition regulation.

MAR.note: Growing deployment for security; limited regulation under data protection framework.

TWN.note: Personal Data Protection Act regulates biometric data; limited police use with civil society oversight and debate.

BLR.note: Facial recognition widely deployed for surveillance of dissidents; used to identify and arrest protesters since 2020.

topics.name: Child Marriage
topics.description: Legal minimum age of marriage and whether exceptions allow minors to marry — from strict 18+ to permitting marriage under 16.

CAN.note: Federal minimum age 16 with consent; several provinces have raised to 18. Civil Marriage Act allows 16+ with parental consent.

USA.note: Varies by state; most states allow marriage at 16-17 with parental consent. Several states have banned child marriage entirely since 2020.

GBR.note: Marriage and Civil Partnership (Minimum Age) Act 2022 raised the minimum to 18 in England and Wales with no exceptions.

FRA.note: Minimum age 18; exceptions possible with parental consent and prosecution authorization in exceptional circumstances.

DEU.note: Minimum age 18 since 2017 law reform; marriages of minors under 16 are void, those between 16-18 can be annulled.

ITA.note: Minimum age 18; juvenile court can authorize marriage at 16 in exceptional cases.

ESP.note: Minimum age 18; emancipated minors at 16 may marry with judicial authorization.

PRT.note: Minimum age 16 with parental consent; under 16 requires judicial authorization.

NLD.note: Minimum age 18 since 2015; previous exception for 16-year-olds with court permission was eliminated.

BEL.note: Minimum age 18 since 2024 reform; no exceptions permitted.

SWE.note: Minimum age 18 with no exceptions since 2014 reform; marriages of minors from abroad not recognized.

NOR.note: Minimum age 18 with no exceptions since 2007; foreign child marriages not recognized.

DNK.note: Minimum age 18 with no exceptions since 2017 reform.

FIN.note: Minimum age 18 since 2019; previous exception allowing Ministry of Justice dispensation was eliminated.

ISL.note: Minimum age 18 with no exceptions.

CHE.note: Minimum age 18 since 2013 reform; no exceptions.

AUT.note: Minimum age 18; exception at 16 with court approval if partner is already of age.

IRL.note: Minimum age 18 under the Domestic Violence Act 2018; previous exemptions removed.

POL.note: Minimum age 18; court may permit marriage at 16 for women in important circumstances (e.g., pregnancy).

CZE.note: Minimum age 18; court may permit marriage at 16 in exceptional circumstances.

HUN.note: Minimum age 18; guardianship authority may permit marriage at 16 in exceptional cases.

ROU.note: Minimum age 18; medical certification can allow marriage at 16. Child marriage prevalent in some Roma communities.

BGR.note: Minimum age 18; marriage at 16 possible with parental consent and regional court approval.

HRV.note: Minimum age 18; court may permit marriage at 16 if it is in the best interest of the minor.

GRC.note: Minimum age 18; court may grant permission at 16 for serious reasons.

TUR.note: Minimum age 18 (17 with parental consent); court may authorize at 16 in exceptional cases. Underage marriage remains prevalent in rural areas.

RUS.note: Minimum age 18; regional authorities may permit marriage at 16 with parental consent. Some regions allow 14-15.

UKR.note: Minimum age 18; court may permit marriage at 16 in exceptional circumstances such as pregnancy.

CHN.note: Minimum age 20 for males and 18 for females under the Marriage Law; strictly enforced.

JPN.note: Minimum age 18 for both sexes since April 2022 Civil Code amendment; previously 16 for women, 18 for men.

KOR.note: Minimum age 18; marriage at 16-17 possible with parental consent under the Civil Act.

IND.note: Minimum age 18 for women, 21 for men under Prohibition of Child Marriage Act. Proposal to equalize at 21 pending. Child marriage remains prevalent.

PAK.note: Minimum age 16 for girls, 18 for boys in most provinces; Sindh raised girls' age to 18. Child marriage widespread despite laws.

BGD.note: Minimum age 18 for women, 21 for men but 2017 law allows exceptions with court and parental approval at any age. One of the highest child marriage rates globally.

IDN.note: Minimum age raised to 19 for both sexes in 2019; court dispensation possible below 19. Child marriage rates remain high.

MYS.note: Minimum age 18 for non-Muslims; Muslim girls can marry at 16 with Sharia court permission, sometimes younger.

SGP.note: Minimum age 18; marriage at 16-17 possible with special license and parental consent.

THA.note: Minimum age 17; court may allow marriage below 17 in exceptional circumstances.

VNM.note: Minimum age 18 for women, 20 for men under the Marriage and Family Law 2014.

PHL.note: Prohibiting Child Marriage Act signed in 2021 sets minimum age at 18 with no exceptions; marriages of minors are void.

AUS.note: Minimum age 18; marriage at 16-17 possible with court approval and parental consent.

NZL.note: Minimum age 18; marriage at 16-17 possible with Family Court consent.

BRA.note: Minimum age 16 with parental consent; marriage under 16 prohibited since 2019 reform removing judicial exception for pregnancy.

ARG.note: Minimum age 18; judicial authorization possible at 16 with parental consent.

MEX.note: All 32 states have set minimum marriage age at 18 with no exceptions as of 2023 reforms.

COL.note: Minimum age 18 since 2024 reform; previously allowed marriage at 14 with parental consent.

CHL.note: Minimum age 18; marriage at 16 possible with parental consent.

ZAF.note: Minimum age 18; marriage at 16-17 possible with parental and ministerial consent. Customary law marriages complicate enforcement.

NGA.note: Child Rights Act sets minimum age at 18 but only adopted in some states; northern states allow marriage at puberty under Islamic law.

EGY.note: Minimum age 18 for both sexes since 2008; enforcement challenges persist, especially in rural areas.

SAU.note: No codified minimum age; marriages reportedly registered for girls as young as 10. Regulations to restrict child marriage under discussion.

ARE.note: No statutory minimum age but Judge's approval required; 2020 Personal Status Law gives courts discretion. Practice is rare.

ISR.note: Minimum age 18; court may permit marriage at 16 in exceptional cases. Higher rates in some communities.

IRN.note: Minimum age 13 for girls and 15 for boys; court can authorize marriage below these ages with guardian consent.

IRQ.note: Minimum age 18; court may permit marriage at 15 in exceptional circumstances. Attempts to lower the age have been contested.

KEN.note: Marriage Act sets minimum age at 18; child marriage remains prevalent in some regions despite legal prohibition.

ETH.note: Minimum age 18 under the Revised Family Code; child marriage widespread in rural areas despite legal prohibition.

MAR.note: Minimum age 18; judicial exceptions at 16 are common and widely criticized by human rights organizations.

TWN.note: Minimum age 18; marriage at 16-17 possible with parental consent under the Civil Code.

BLR.note: Minimum age 18; marriage at 15-17 possible with local executive committee approval in exceptional cases.

topics.name: Whistleblower Protection
topics.description: How strongly countries protect people who report wrongdoing — from comprehensive legal shields to criminalization of disclosure.

CAN.note: Public Servants Disclosure Protection Act covers federal employees; private sector protections vary by province. Gaps in coverage noted.

USA.note: Multiple federal laws including Whistleblower Protection Act, Dodd-Frank, and Sarbanes-Oxley; SEC whistleblower program has paid billions in awards.

GBR.note: Public Interest Disclosure Act 1998 provides strong protections; Employment Rights Act prohibits retaliation. Recent reforms proposed to strengthen.

FRA.note: Sapin II Law (2016) and 2022 transposition of EU Directive provide comprehensive whistleblower protection with dedicated channels.

DEU.note: Hinweisgeberschutzgesetz (Whistleblower Protection Act) 2023 transposed EU Directive; comprehensive protection for internal and external reporting.

ITA.note: Legislative Decree 24/2023 transposed EU Whistleblower Directive; ANAC oversees protections. Recent reforms strengthened framework.

ESP.note: Law 2/2023 transposed EU Whistleblower Directive; established Independent Authority for Whistleblower Protection.

PRT.note: Law 93/2021 transposed EU Whistleblower Directive; protections cover both public and private sector disclosures.

NLD.note: Whistleblowers Protection Act updated in 2023 to transpose EU Directive; House for Whistleblowers provides investigation and advice.

BEL.note: Federal law transposing EU Whistleblower Directive enacted in 2022; protections cover both public and private sectors.

SWE.note: New whistleblower protection law in 2021 transposing EU Directive; builds on strong tradition of constitutional press source protection.

NOR.note: Working Environment Act provides comprehensive whistleblower protections; strengthened by 2020 amendments.

DNK.note: Whistleblower Protection Act 2021 transposing EU Directive provides comprehensive protections; covers organizations with 50+ employees.

FIN.note: Whistleblower Protection Act 2023 transposed EU Directive; covers reporting of EU law breaches.

ISL.note: Whistleblower protection provisions in the Administrative Procedures Act; limited compared to EU standards.

CHE.note: No comprehensive whistleblower protection law; Code of Obligations provides limited protections. Multiple legislative attempts have failed.

AUT.note: HSchG (Whistleblower Protection Act) 2023 transposed EU Directive; covers public and private sector.

IRL.note: Protected Disclosures Act 2014 (amended 2022) provides comprehensive protection; considered among the strongest frameworks globally.

POL.note: Whistleblower protection law transposing EU Directive enacted in 2024 after delays; covers internal and external reporting.

CZE.note: Whistleblower Protection Act 2023 transposed EU Directive; protections cover public and private sector.

HUN.note: Complaint handling law provides basic framework; EU Directive transposition delayed. Government critics face retaliation in practice.

ROU.note: Law 361/2022 transposed EU Whistleblower Directive; existing 2004 whistleblower law updated.

BGR.note: Law transposing EU Whistleblower Directive enacted in 2023; implementation capacity remains limited.

HRV.note: Whistleblower Protection Act 2019 predated EU Directive; updated in 2022 for full compliance.

GRC.note: Law 4990/2022 transposed EU Whistleblower Directive; National Transparency Authority provides oversight.

TUR.note: Limited sectoral protections for anti-corruption reporting; no comprehensive whistleblower protection law.

RUS.note: No whistleblower protection law; reporting wrongdoing, especially regarding government, carries significant retaliation risk.

UKR.note: Whistleblower protection law enacted in 2019; protections strengthened but enforcement limited by war and institutional capacity.

CHN.note: No independent whistleblower protection; reporting through official complaint channels exists but retaliation is common. State secrets laws discourage disclosure.

JPN.note: Whistleblower Protection Act revised in 2022 to strengthen protections; requires companies with 300+ employees to establish reporting channels.

KOR.note: Act on Protection of Public Interest Whistleblowers provides strong protections including financial rewards; Anti-Corruption and Civil Rights Commission handles cases.

IND.note: Whistle Blowers Protection Act 2014 exists but implementation rules not fully notified; RTI activists face threats and violence.

PAK.note: Limited protections under certain anti-corruption laws; no comprehensive whistleblower protection framework.

BGD.note: No dedicated whistleblower protection law; Digital Security Act used to penalize disclosures. Significant retaliation risk.

IDN.note: Witness and Victim Protection Agency provides some protections; limited coverage and enforcement capacity.

MYS.note: Whistleblower Protection Act 2010 exists but scope is limited; protections apply only if disclosure is made to enforcement agencies.

SGP.note: No comprehensive whistleblower protection law; some sectoral protections exist. Companies Act has limited provisions.

THA.note: Limited protections under witness protection law; no comprehensive whistleblower framework.

VNM.note: Denunciation Law provides theoretical protections but enforcement is weak; whistleblowers face significant retaliation risk.

PHL.note: Witness Protection Act provides limited coverage; no comprehensive whistleblower protection framework.

AUS.note: Treasury Laws Amendment (Enhancing Whistleblower Protections) Act 2019 provides comprehensive protection for corporate whistleblowers.

NZL.note: Protected Disclosures (Protection of Whistleblowers) Act 2022 provides comprehensive protection; replaced 2000 law.

BRA.note: Anti-corruption legislation provides some whistleblower incentives; CGU complaint channel exists but comprehensive protection lacking.

ARG.note: Limited whistleblower protection provisions in anti-corruption law; no comprehensive framework.

MEX.note: General Law of Administrative Responsibilities provides some protections for corruption reporting; enforcement limited.

COL.note: Anti-corruption statute provides limited whistleblower protections; protection program exists but capacity constrained.

CHL.note: Law 21.595 (2023) introduced whistleblower protections for economic crimes; broader framework emerging.

ZAF.note: Protected Disclosures Act 2000 provides framework; enforcement and protection from retaliation remain challenges.

NGA.note: Whistleblower Policy launched in 2016 incentivizes financial crime reporting; limited legal protections against retaliation.

EGY.note: No whistleblower protection law; reporting wrongdoing can lead to prosecution or retaliation. NGO law restricts civil society.

SAU.note: No independent whistleblower protections; reporting through official channels exists but no protection from retaliation.

ARE.note: Limited protections in some sectoral laws; Dubai Financial Services Authority has whistleblower provisions.

ISR.note: Protection of Workers (Exposure of Offenses) Law provides protections for employees reporting violations; courts have upheld protections.

IRN.note: No whistleblower protection; disclosure of government misconduct can result in severe penalties.

IRQ.note: No effective whistleblower protections; corruption whistleblowers face threats and violence.

KEN.note: Witness Protection Act provides some coverage; Kenya Witness Protection Agency has limited capacity and resources.

ETH.note: Proclamation on Whistleblowers and Witnesses Protection provides basic framework; enforcement limited.

MAR.note: Whistleblower protection provisions in anti-corruption law; enforcement and cultural barriers limit effectiveness.

TWN.note: Witness Protection Act and sector-specific laws provide protections; Whistleblower Protection Act passed in 2024.

BLR.note: No whistleblower protections; reporting government wrongdoing carries severe risk of criminal prosecution and retaliation.

topics.name: Hate Speech Regulation
topics.description: How countries balance hate speech prohibition with freedom of expression — from strict criminal penalties to no hate speech laws.

scale.label: No Hate Speech Laws (Free Speech Priority)

CAN.note: Criminal Code sections 318-320 criminalize hate propaganda and incitement; 2024 Online Harms Act expanded protections.

USA.note: First Amendment broadly protects speech including hate speech; only speech inciting imminent lawless action is unprotected under Brandenburg v. Ohio.

GBR.note: Public Order Act, Racial and Religious Hatred Act, and Online Safety Act criminalize hate speech; enhanced sentencing for hate-motivated offenses.

FRA.note: Loi Pleven criminalizes incitement to hatred; Holocaust denial law (Gayssot Act); Online hate speech law strengthened. Strict enforcement.

DEU.note: Volksverhetzung (incitement to hatred) under Section 130 StGB; Holocaust denial criminalized. Network Enforcement Act requires online platform action.

ITA.note: Mancino Law criminalizes incitement to racial, ethnic, and religious hatred; penalties of up to 4 years imprisonment.

ESP.note: Penal Code Article 510 criminalizes incitement to hatred with up to 4 years imprisonment; broad coverage of protected characteristics.

PRT.note: Penal Code criminalizes incitement to hatred based on race, religion, and other grounds; enforcement has increased.

NLD.note: Articles 137c-137e criminalize hate speech; high-profile prosecutions including politicians. Active enforcement.

BEL.note: Anti-racism Law and Anti-discrimination Law criminalize incitement to hatred; Unia equality body handles complaints.

SWE.note: Criminal Code Chapter 16 Section 8 criminalizes agitation against a national or ethnic group; convictions include social media posts.

NOR.note: Penal Code Section 185 criminalizes hate speech based on ethnicity, religion, sexuality; moderate enforcement.

DNK.note: Penal Code Section 266b criminalizes hate speech; narrow scope focused on public statements. Active but measured enforcement.

FIN.note: Criminal Code criminalizes ethnic agitation; prosecutions for online hate speech have increased.

ISL.note: General Penal Code Article 233a criminalizes public hate speech based on nationality, ethnicity, religion, sexuality.

CHE.note: Anti-Racism Norm (Article 261bis) criminalizes incitement to hatred; extended to sexual orientation in 2020 referendum.

AUT.note: Verbotsgesetz prohibits Nazi glorification; Penal Code Section 283 criminalizes incitement to hatred against protected groups.

IRL.note: Criminal Justice (Incitement to Violence or Hatred and Hate Offences) Act 2023 updated framework; replaces 1989 law.

POL.note: Penal Code Article 256-257 criminalizes incitement to hatred based on national, ethnic, racial, or religious grounds.

CZE.note: Criminal Code Section 356 criminalizes incitement to hatred; includes online speech. Enforcement moderate.

HUN.note: Criminal Code criminalizes incitement against communities; enforcement inconsistent. Government rhetoric sometimes contradicts anti-hate speech norms.

ROU.note: Emergency Ordinance 31/2002 criminalizes fascist, racist, and xenophobic symbols and speech; Holocaust denial criminalized.

BGR.note: Penal Code criminalizes incitement to hatred; enforcement limited. EU framework compliance in place.

HRV.note: Criminal Code criminalizes public incitement to violence and hatred; covers multiple protected grounds.

GRC.note: Anti-racism law 4285/2014 criminalizes public incitement to violence or hatred based on protected characteristics.

TUR.note: Penal Code Article 216 criminalizes incitement to hatred; applied broadly but selectively, sometimes against government critics.

RUS.note: Criminal Code Article 282 criminalizes incitement to hatred; broadly applied and often used to suppress political dissent and minority expression.

UKR.note: Criminal Code criminalizes incitement to ethnic and religious hatred; enforcement capacity affected by war.

CHN.note: Laws criminalize speech promoting ethnic hatred; applied selectively. Government controls speech broadly under national security and social harmony frameworks.

JPN.note: Hate Speech Elimination Act 2016 declares hate speech unacceptable but imposes no criminal penalties; local ordinances in Osaka and Kawasaki go further.

KOR.note: No comprehensive hate speech law; National Human Rights Commission handles discrimination complaints. Legislative proposals have stalled.

IND.note: IPC Section 153A criminalizes promoting enmity between groups; Section 295A punishes insulting religion. Enforcement inconsistent.

PAK.note: Blasphemy laws carry severe penalties including death; broader hate speech provisions exist but enforcement is selective.

BGD.note: Digital Security Act criminalizes speech hurting religious sentiments; blasphemy-related provisions in Penal Code.

IDN.note: Criminal Code and Electronic Information Law criminalize incitement to hatred and blasphemy; ITE Law broadly applied.

MYS.note: Sedition Act and Communications and Multimedia Act criminalize speech inciting hatred; applied to racial and religious speech.

SGP.note: Maintenance of Religious Harmony Act and Penal Code strictly regulate speech on race and religion; Protection from Online Falsehoods Act adds enforcement tools.

THA.note: Criminal Code provisions on incitement; lèse-majesté law (Section 112) broadly criminalizes speech about monarchy.

VNM.note: Penal Code criminalizes speech inciting ethnic hatred; broader speech restrictions limit all forms of dissent.

PHL.note: No comprehensive hate speech law; Revised Penal Code has limited provisions on incitement. Anti-discrimination bills pending.

AUS.note: Racial Discrimination Act Section 18C prohibits racial vilification; criminal penalties in state laws. Religious vilification covered in some states.

NZL.note: Human Rights Act prohibits incitement to racial disharmony; Christchurch attack prompted proposals to strengthen hate speech laws.

BRA.note: Constitution and Penal Code criminalize racism and incitement to racial discrimination; 2023 law equated homophobia with racism.

ARG.note: Anti-Discrimination Law 23.592 provides criminal penalties for hate speech; INADI handles complaints.

MEX.note: Federal Law to Prevent and Eliminate Discrimination provides civil remedies; no specific criminal hate speech law.

COL.note: Anti-discrimination law criminalizes acts of discrimination and harassment based on protected characteristics.

CHL.note: Zamudio Law (2012) criminalizes violence motivated by discrimination; covers hate crimes rather than hate speech specifically.

ZAF.note: Promotion of Equality and Prevention of Unfair Discrimination Act prohibits hate speech; Prevention of Hate Crimes Bill pending.

NGA.note: Limited hate speech provisions; Cybercrimes Act has some provisions. Proposals for hate speech regulation have been controversial.

EGY.note: Penal Code criminalizes incitement to discrimination; blasphemy provisions strictly enforced. Laws selectively applied.

SAU.note: Anti-terrorism law and cybercrime regulations broadly criminalize speech deemed hateful; blasphemy harshly punished. Applied selectively.

ARE.note: Federal Decree-Law 2/2015 on combating discrimination and hatred imposes strict penalties; broad definition includes online speech.

ISR.note: Prohibition of Incitement to Racism Law 1986 criminalizes racist speech; enforcement moderate.

IRN.note: Penal Code criminalizes insults to Islam and religious figures; broadly applied to suppress dissent and minority expression.

IRQ.note: Penal Code criminalizes incitement to sectarian violence; blasphemy provisions exist. Enforcement inconsistent.

KEN.note: National Cohesion and Integration Act criminalizes hate speech and ethnic incitement; National Cohesion and Integration Commission handles cases.

ETH.note: Hate Speech and Disinformation Prevention Proclamation 2020 imposes strict criminal penalties for hate speech; up to 5 years imprisonment.

MAR.note: Penal Code criminalizes incitement to racial hatred and discrimination; press code has provisions on hate speech.

TWN.note: No specific hate speech law; Anti-discrimination provisions exist in some sectoral laws. Freedom of speech prioritized.

BLR.note: Criminal Code criminalizes incitement to racial and ethnic hatred; used broadly against political dissent.

topics.name: Mandatory Voting
topics.description: Whether citizens are required by law to vote in elections — from strictly enforced compulsory voting to restricted suffrage.

CAN.note: Voluntary voting; turnout typically 60-70% in federal elections. Universal suffrage for citizens 18+.

USA.note: Voluntary voting; turnout varies widely. Voter registration requirements and ID laws vary by state. Universal suffrage but felon disenfranchisement varies.

GBR.note: Voluntary voting; turnout around 60-67% in general elections. Universal suffrage for citizens and qualifying Commonwealth residents 18+.

FRA.note: Voluntary voting; turnout declining in recent elections. Universal suffrage for citizens 18+.

DEU.note: Voluntary voting; turnout typically 70-77% in federal elections. Universal suffrage for citizens 18+.

ITA.note: Voting is described as a 'civic duty' in the Constitution but penalties for non-voting were abolished in 1993.

ESP.note: Voluntary voting; turnout typically 65-75% in general elections. Universal suffrage for citizens 18+.

PRT.note: Voluntary voting; turnout relatively low at 50-60%. Universal suffrage for citizens 18+.

NLD.note: Voluntary since 1970 (compulsory voting abolished); turnout typically 75-82%. Universal suffrage for citizens 18+.

BEL.note: Compulsory voting since 1893; enforced with potential fines for non-voters. Turnout consistently above 85%.

SWE.note: Voluntary voting; turnout exceptionally high at 80-87% despite no compulsion. Universal suffrage for citizens 18+.

NOR.note: Voluntary voting; turnout typically 75-80%. Universal suffrage for citizens 18+.

DNK.note: Voluntary voting; turnout consistently high at 80-86%. Universal suffrage for citizens 18+.

FIN.note: Voluntary voting; turnout typically 68-73% in parliamentary elections. Universal suffrage for citizens 18+.

ISL.note: Voluntary voting; turnout typically 75-82%. Universal suffrage for citizens 18+.

CHE.note: Voluntary voting federally (Schaffhausen canton has compulsory voting); turnout low at 40-50%. Universal suffrage.

AUT.note: Voluntary since 2004 (compulsory voting at state level abolished); turnout typically 75-80%.

IRL.note: Voluntary voting; turnout typically 60-65%. Universal suffrage for citizens and residents 18+.

POL.note: Voluntary voting; turnout increasing in recent elections (74% in 2023). Universal suffrage for citizens 18+.

CZE.note: Voluntary voting; turnout typically 60-70%. Universal suffrage for citizens 18+.

HUN.note: Voluntary voting; turnout typically 65-70%. Universal suffrage for citizens 18+.

ROU.note: Voluntary voting; turnout low at 30-50%. Universal suffrage for citizens 18+.

BGR.note: Compulsory voting reintroduced in 2016 but enforcement is minimal; turnout remains low.

HRV.note: Voluntary voting; turnout typically 50-65%. Universal suffrage for citizens 18+.

GRC.note: Compulsory voting in law but not enforced; penalties have not been imposed. Turnout 55-65%.

TUR.note: Compulsory voting with nominal fines for non-voters; turnout typically 85-90%.

RUS.note: Voluntary voting; officially reported turnout varies. Elections criticized for limited genuine competition and electoral manipulation.

UKR.note: Voluntary voting; turnout varies by election. Martial law has suspended elections since 2022.

CHN.note: Elections only at local People's Congress level with CPC-approved candidates; no competitive multiparty elections. Effectively restricted suffrage.

JPN.note: Voluntary voting; turnout low at 50-56% in recent elections. Universal suffrage for citizens 18+ (lowered from 20 in 2016).

KOR.note: Voluntary voting; turnout typically 65-78%. Universal suffrage for citizens 18+.

IND.note: Voluntary voting; turnout typically 65-70% in general elections. Universal suffrage for citizens 18+. Some states have debated compulsory voting.

PAK.note: Voluntary voting; turnout typically 50-55%. Universal suffrage for citizens 18+.

BGD.note: Voluntary voting; turnout varies widely. Universal suffrage for citizens 18+.

IDN.note: Voluntary voting; turnout typically 70-80% due to strong civic culture. Universal suffrage for citizens 17+ or married.

MYS.note: Voluntary voting; automatic voter registration since 2022. Universal suffrage for citizens 18+ (lowered from 21).

SGP.note: Compulsory voting enforced; non-voters removed from electoral register and must pay fee to be reinstated. Turnout above 93%.

THA.note: Compulsory voting under the Constitution; non-voters face restrictions on running for office and some public positions. Lightly enforced.

VNM.note: Elections held but only Vietnamese Fatherland Front-approved candidates; single-party state. Effectively restricted suffrage.

PHL.note: Voluntary voting; turnout typically 75-82%. Universal suffrage for citizens 18+.

AUS.note: Compulsory voting since 1924; enforced with fines (AUD 20-50). Turnout consistently above 90%.

NZL.note: Voluntary voting but voter registration is compulsory; turnout typically 75-82%. Universal suffrage for citizens and permanent residents 18+.

BRA.note: Compulsory voting for literate citizens 18-70; enforced with fines and restrictions on public services. Turnout 78-80%.

ARG.note: Compulsory voting for citizens 18-70; enforced with fines. Turnout typically 75-82%.

MEX.note: Compulsory voting in the Constitution but not enforced; no penalties for non-voting. Turnout typically 60-65%.

COL.note: Voluntary voting; turnout typically 50-55%. Universal suffrage for citizens 18+. Draft bills for compulsory voting debated.

CHL.note: Voluntary voting since 2012 (previously compulsory); turnout dropped significantly after change. Automatic voter registration.

ZAF.note: Voluntary voting; turnout has been declining from 86% in 1994 to 59% in 2024.

NGA.note: Voluntary voting; turnout very low at 25-35% in recent elections. Universal suffrage for citizens 18+.

EGY.note: Compulsory voting with nominal fine for non-voting; rarely enforced. Elections criticized for limited competition.

SAU.note: Municipal elections only (since 2005); women gained suffrage in 2015. No parliamentary elections; governance by monarchy.

ARE.note: Federal National Council elections use limited electoral college; only selected citizens may vote. No universal suffrage for national legislature.

ISR.note: Voluntary voting; turnout typically 67-72%. Universal suffrage for citizens 18+.

IRN.note: Voluntary voting; candidates must be approved by Guardian Council. Turnout has declined in recent elections due to disillusionment.

IRQ.note: Voluntary voting; turnout has varied from 41-62% in recent elections. Universal suffrage for citizens 18+.

KEN.note: Voluntary voting; turnout typically 60-65%. Universal suffrage for citizens 18+.

ETH.note: Voluntary voting; 2021 elections held under difficult conditions. Universal suffrage for citizens 18+.

MAR.note: Voluntary voting; turnout low at 30-50%. Universal suffrage for citizens 18+.

TWN.note: Voluntary voting; turnout typically 65-75%. Universal suffrage for citizens 20+ (lowered to 18 debated).

BLR.note: Elections held but widely considered unfree; Lukashenko has controlled results since 1994. Opposition candidates face imprisonment.
